speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Edward Groseclose and Dixie Ball Groseclose took place at their home in Juliaetta on July 21st, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know I was going to ask you a little bit about a couple of your kin down there at Arrow who you'd mentioned to me and I'd also heard about were pretty interesting people and one of them was Aunt Susie.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Aunt Susan?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah. Yeah. I was wondering what you remember about her, what she was like.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I mean what she was like. Well, I had the idea she was quite a lady is what I heard.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, she was quite an old lady to get around and do and go places and take care of people in the sick. Oh,

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I don't know, she was just a general old lady of them times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember her when you were first here? She was still around, wasn't she? Oh, yeah, she, oh I don't know, can't remember what year that she died, but I can remember just about a year or less after we came to Idaho, in 1970, 1980, or 1980 rather, and she was a neighbor, what was it, four miles from us?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: About four miles away. Yeah.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: A mile away, apart from our folks, and they had a big family and lived on a homestead ranch up there just below the George Johnson place down the canyon.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, that steep hill out this side of George Johnson's there, where them blackberries up on the hill and across, that was their homestead there.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Out from their homestead on the track, or just below the track, they had kind of a garden out there. She clamped up ground cherries instead of the blackberries, and later come on, in the near years that she put ground hogs, ground cherries in. And she was just a big worker, she done lots of sewing and crocheting, had her a good floor and garden, and the general all around person always very like that.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And they had their garden out there below the railroad tracks where all them blackberries is, and they had to pack their stuff to the house from there. One time she was out there. About a quarter of a mile.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: She was out there, was together in ground cherries, wasn't it, when she found the dead man.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh yeah, found a man dead out there.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I guess he was kind of a, what they call a hobo or somebody, you know, and a cramp out there. And so she found the...

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Early days, and people used to, hobos they called them, they'd go along and on, and where they got tired or anything, they'd stop them. I guess this hobo was sick and he was led down there on the river and died.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you see many hobos?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh yes, in the early days, there was a lot of them went along and they had to bomb for something to eat at different places.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: There was one especially back in the East, and anybody come to your place, you took them in the house. Rainy country back there, and they never approached a fella to stay outside, but this country here, well that was... You had all the outside to sleep in, and that's where you stayed. But they were a hobo come through the country here and bombed around for quite a while, and my mother and my brother Grover was staying at my brother Will's place in 1904 when he went...

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: You told that before, didn't you?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Not to me.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Didn't you tell about the bomb that had the lice?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Uh -uh. So he come up there and they fed him, but he went away and then at Christmas time, my brother, he come up to rest of my brothers up in the potlatch, and I went down there and stayed with my mother, so he come back then when there was no... Me and my mother there, and I was just, well I was ten years old I guess. He come in, sit down there, and after, oh, ten o 'clock or so, she'd ask him if he was going to go to... Well, maybe he would. So she took him up, showed him upstairs where to go to bed. He went up there, and next day he got up, come around, stayed around again. I went out and cut wood, and he great big 200 pound fella, he stood there and watched me cut wood, never off the cut any. She got to watch now at the door, see what he was doing on. Well, I was cutting wood, he was standing there with his shirt open.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, like that, she seen what was... She seen then he was lousy, she knowed what she was up for. He stayed there till she sent me with a note up to a neighbor Hickman up there to have him come down and tell him to leave. She wrote in a note the letters were there, and she said he'd been dead three days and wanted Eli to come down and take care of him, bury him or something. But while I was gone up there, Grover and my other brother, Hughes, they come down. So they was having quite an argument when I got back down there. They give him the dinner, and he'd get kind of cranky with Hughes, but he never would. I go to Grover, and Grover gets kind of cranky with him and says, I think it was your place here a while back, he says. You and I were very good, pretty good friends. But he went away, he'd never come back. We had to clean up them lives.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did he stay?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh, he come there sometime the day along down to noon sometime the one day and stayed till afternoon the next before he left. They had to invite him to leave before he went.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your mother didn't feel she could just tell him to get out?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I don't know what a little old woman thought them days, a big husky guy come along and stopped the other way.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Back to Aunt Susan, why she always was busy as long as she lived. I can remember why she'd work all day and then in the evening she'd sit down and she always had her mending basket or piece of quilts or crocheting, fixing, sewing, making something for some child or something that way. So, I don't know, she finally broke her hip and was in a wheelchair for a while. So, you'd go in, she was never idle no matter what. She'd have three or four crocheting things working on them. You know, she'd be kind of tired of one pattern and fool with another one. And I used to always tell her that if I ever got to heaven that I'd sure glance around. If I saw somebody over in the corner with their hands applying, I'd go to the corner because I know it was her. She's so busy all the time. And one time my mother was quite ill. I remember she had pneumonia at 12. And anyway, they were setting up with her at night. And she'd work all day and get on a horse and ride down and set up with my daughter all night and then in the morning get on her horse and go home and see about her family. And one time my dad brought the horse out of the barn for her to get on to go home. He was holding the horse and it was kind of a side hill life. It wasn't too big of a horse. And she was a pretty active lady at that time. So she grabbed a hold of the saddle horn and gave a jump to jump up onto the saddle. And she went clear over the horse, hung onto the saddle horn and she jumped and she went clear on the other side instead of landing on the saddle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she get hurt at all?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, it didn't hurt her. Kind of twisted her arm a little hanging on. But she just laughed. She was always quite jolly and jokey about everything. She had a daughter and all the kids would go there quite a lot. And we were on about 12, 14 in the air. And her daughter, Anna May, was beginning to run in the soul a little bit. And so she's making her a nightgown. Her mother offered to help her but Anna May decided that she didn't want any help. She wanted to do it all by herself. And she did just fine until she came to making her buttonholes. And so she put her buttons on and made her buttonholes. So her mother laughed so about it that she got the gown out and showed us when we went to visit. And instead of folding that cloth, you know, and just cutting a little slit to make the buttonholes, Anna May cut little round circles for her buttonholes and she worked her little round circles. And her mother just laughed and laughed over that, showed everybody at Calum about Anna May's buttonholes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Anna May embarrassed by that, you think? You think the girl would be embarrassed by that?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, it didn't bother Anna May too much, you know. Her mother just got such a bang out of it all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that, was Aunt Susan called Aunt by everybody or just by her nephews?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, she seemed to go by that name. Everybody called her Aunt Susan. There were so many growth courses around, you know. That was Aunt Susan and Uncle Jim and Aunt Liza and Big Jim and Little Jim and all of that. But my brothers, you know, when they were little, they liked to go to visit Aunt Susan and Uncle Jim. And they were small and they'd say, let's go to Susan Jim's. And they'd make the folks to go up to Jim's and stay all day. Susan Jim's. I kind of want to go to Susan Jim's. But Aunt Susan was just, two of the nieces had been nephews and everybody that come along was Aunt Susan, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why would they want to spend the day at her place? Why would they want to spend the day at her place?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, they had kids that were smaller, you know, and just jolly... Friends. They had little friends.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: But she worked harder than the average woman. Well, I don't know that she actually did. They all had a lot of families, but I don't think she was any harder worker than some of the others.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, I don't know. She was huskier alone about that time than the others around. She had that family of nine to look after. Well, I don't know.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: After I growed up, I said to Dad Susan and my mother were two of the meanest women that ever lived. They raised a whole bunch of boys that never know anything. They didn't really know anything about doing housework. We never had to wash dishes. We never cleaned up the house. We didn't know nothing about that. No. Well, Mom, our mother's done that. Had some cousins that come down here from Spokane.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And they had nine kids too, but...

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: The little boys, we got to be eight or nine years old, they had to do the dishes. The older boys, well, the oldest one, he was a bellhop from Spokane. I was there when the folks were away and the first thing he done before he got dinner was to go and take his broom and clean the whole house. Clean it all up good and then he could make a good dinner. I seen that there. I seen, well, them people. Them people learnt their boys something, but my mother and Aunt Susan, they just waited on us boys like, I don't know what they did like.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the girls? Did the girls work?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh, yeah, the girls, they worked. They done the housework. He only had one sister himself. I had one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But Aunt Susan had four girls, wasn't it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Three. Three girls, Minnie, Adam, May, and Dessie.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you must have enjoyed that when you were growing up not to have to do any of that stuff, didn't you?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: But when it got a little older and we had to get out on our own a little bit, it didn't come in. It wasn't so good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You didn't know how to batch?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, I went out, well, when I was seventeen, my sister and I were sixteen, we went up in the hills and milked cows, took her. Took all the cattle up here in the hills from Teakin. And when I was seventeen, I went up there and my sister got too thick. She had a filled tooth, ulcerated, and there was nothing that would ease it but cold water in her mouth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know how long that would ease it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: That's all she could find. Well, she started out the next day and my brother, a preacher there, he had to come out to preach, so they thought they'd get a tooth pulled at Southwick. There was nobody there and she had to go clear to Kendrick. Then she was down in this country and when she got down there, she stayed down there two weeks. I was up there alone with, see I had eleven cows to milk and ten calves to feed and a separator to clean up and do my own cooking. Carry the water that I'd drunk and wash stuff up three -quarters of a mile in a bucket for two weeks. And bring in my cows. Oh, I knowed how to stir up bread, but the one batch, the last batch I made, I forgot to put any salt in it. I would get up for morning and go and milk. Take the cows and get them out, send them to the calves, and then I'd get my breakfast over about ten o 'clock. Lay down and get things cleaned up about ten, then lay down and sleep for about three. Get up and go and right after the cows, there were no fences. You pointed the cows north, you expected to go north to find them. If they circled and went south, maybe you didn't find them for a day or two. So this evening I come in and my oldest brother and his wife and my sister come in for a team. My brother got sick. My sister had to drive the team in. She hadn't been used to driving four horses. She was good with two, but she'd come in for that outfit. I got in and I had nothing to eat. They'd eat everything up. My sister -in -law, she said that was the best bread she'd ever eat. Well, she'd been out all day without nothing to eat. I laughed at her. I told her I could beat her cooking. She stayed there for a couple of weeks, and boy, if she didn't do her best, she'd put cream in her biscuits. She'd have biscuits you could hardly pick up that would nearly fall into pieces. But I never would brag on her, only that was pretty good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She was doing that because you'd been doing all the cooking by yourself? She was making up for it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I'd been doing the cooking and she wanted me to brag on her cooking. She could cook all right, but then she could slop it up too if she wanted to. Didn't try all the time to cook, but that time she was doing her best.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think the idea was that your mother and your aunt didn't teach the boys?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, I don't know. I guess back up to their time, why that was the way it was done. They were a little older than this other family, and this other family was raised in town. I guess that's the way all the old -timers done. The men done work outside the house and the women done the inside work. If they didn't have enough to do that, they'd get a hired girl to help. I guess old -timers didn't do much, only when they had to go out on the hills hunting or something like that, or some camps.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: The women didn't often go out in the fields and help like they did in later years. Maybe spin and weave and sew and cook and can.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, they did go out. My mother used to go out in the fields and drop beans.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When she was young?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, well, she was young, yes, after I could remember those. I was the youngest one. She wasn't very young.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: But you're saying that it was the... Oh, my folks, I can't remember my aunts and all of them, you know, and the granddad's family, doing very much outside work. But they would, you know, help together in the fruit or something. But then when they got big enough, they'd go and do housework for somebody else, you know, to earn money. But I can't remember them just going out in the fields and taking a part and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you figure that the outside work was more later for women, more?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: It seemed to me like it is. More and more women's lib.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what about you? Were you raised to do just the inside work for the most part?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, not entirely. My main chore at home was really bringing in the cows and milking and things like that. I didn't do an awful lot of garden work.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: You didn't do much field and garden work.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I married pretty young, and then after I was married, I loved to work outdoors. I still don't like housework. I don't like any part of it. I used to like to go out and shock hay, and I had hay back in the barn, and they hauled it in. I never did do much mowing, but I did look quite a lot of raking and shocking and hauling and all like that. I guess I didn't understand enough. Maybe I'd run into something with the mower blade, but I tried it a little bit. Even tried plowing a little, but I didn't get very far with that because of cutting and covering, but I just thought it was fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because what?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I plowed. You said cut and cover. I cut and covered. I mean, you know, you just get in and out. You couldn't keep the furrow going like he did, and I'd get real aggravated because when our oldest daughter was a baby, I went out. I was lonesome, you know, out in the field where he was plowing, following along after watching him plow, and I asked him to let me plow. He could carry the baby, and so he let me try and all. I would get pretty aggravated because I couldn't do it as good as he could.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, that's a funny thing about them old foot burner plows. The man that used to run them, he noticed that every move that plow makes, and it's just adjusted like that, you know. But the first time you start to plow, if that plow starts to go too far, take too much land, well, you don't notice it until it's got over there four or five inches. Then you throw it back, and when you throw it back, most likely you'll throw it too far, and then it'll be going this way when it goes this way, but it won't cut it all out. It's just so that they're over, and then that's what they call cutting and covering. When you go good, and then you press down the handle a little too much, it'll come up, and you make dog beds.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Dog beds?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, dog beds in there you plow. Plow don't run level. It'll go up and down, you know, and make a deep place in the shallow. Yeah, it's quite a thing to that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's what was happening to you?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: The cutting and covering.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I didn't plow very far along the strip, but it looks so easy to walk along and see him going, and it's just smooth. I don't know. You never seem to think, after you got used to running that plow, if it started to move, if it started to go too far that way, well, you just back, you know. Well, it was all done within an inch. If that tipped an inch, well, you'd throw that back an inch. You held it right there and just let it go. And if you had a plow sharpened right, you could set it in the ground and turn it loose and it would run. But if it wasn't, well, then you had to keep fighting it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, listen, if you didn't like to do the housework kind of stuff and the cooking so much, you still must have had to do an awful lot of it in those days, didn't you?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, when I was home having the younger brothers, you know, the one sister, the kids, I was eight years old, and then the rest of them, and I had to do a lot of work, a lot of babysitting, a lot of water carrying, and quite a bit of washing and dishwashing and cooking. Of course, unless Mama was sick, I didn't do such a lot of the cooking, but he was pretty good to help with the cooking. And she cooked because we never had a lot on hand and couldn't afford to waste anything or fix something nobody could eat. So, but I had to do a lot of preparing and getting things in and I don't know, I was quite busy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think maybe that's why you didn't like it so much, because you had to do so much when you were young, when you were a kid?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I don't know, but I just naturally, now with my youngest, well my only sister, my mother could knit and crochet and sew and all that, but I never cared for any of that. My sis was like that. She could do all kinds of fancy work and I could sew a little bit or a piece of quilt, but I don't like to be very particular or anything fancy. I think it was beautiful to see it pretty, but I'd rather have a magazine or a book or out playing along the river, or going that way than I would for having a fishing pole and I wouldn't have a needle in my hand or crocheting it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you say to me before that you missed, had to miss a lot of school because of your mother being sick?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, I missed a lot. I don't know whether I'd have done away with my report cards, but I used to have quite a few of them. Oh, out of the seven, eight months term, I probably wouldn't average attendance of what would figure four months out of a term in the school room. You know, I'd be missing so much through sickness in the family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But it wasn't usually your being sick so much as your mother, right?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, I didn't miss a lot on my being sick, but mostly some of the rest of the family, you know, and helping with the little ones and with my mother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you feel that retarded your learning? Do you think that that held back your learning a lot to miss so much school? I mean, could you catch up with that kind of thing easy?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I could do pretty good. I got along good with school. I liked school. And even some of the teachers tried to encourage me to go on with school, but financial -wise, the folks, I had to stay away from home and all, and they didn't agree to it. But I did manage to make my grade each year, so I made a grade a year in spite of it all, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember how you felt about that then? I mean, did you feel that you were really missing out to not be able to go to school all the time?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, I didn't feel bad that I couldn't go. I don't remember that I really felt that I was being held back. Seemed like some way I could understand that I was needed. But I liked to go until it made me feel real bad to not be able to go more and to be in school. But I just, I don't know, seemed like I more or less accepted it as something that had to be. I always kind of felt that I did. And when I look back on the things that I did, I learned an awful lot of lessons, and there's quite a bit that helped me. After I was married, my life went on, and being poor people, when I look back on it, I think that I learned something from all of it, you know, that was helpful in my years later.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you mean, when you say you learned something, are you thinking of like you learned how to care for people?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, yeah, I think of more understanding of people, and how to be content to get along with what you have. Like that, you know, just kind of accept what comes and not expect to be a millionaire when you have only a nickel.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your mother sick chronically?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, no, not so much. The babies come pretty fast, and then she had ties off and on, there was kidney problems and pneumonia, and just things that happened along. But then, oh, she finally had a stroke, and that was several years after I was married. She passed away when she was only sixty -four, and it must have been it, you know. She hit her in the morning, and she was never conscious till night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But when you were helping, when you were staying out of school, was it mostly when she was actually sick or just when you were needed to help raise the kids?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, whenever she, just whenever I was needed to help with the youngsters, and little things that I could do, you know, the neighbors would come in and help what they could with the work. But it was just somebody to be in the house and all the time to help look after the smaller ones when the dad had to be outside, you know, and things. It was just that I was, well, back then, an eight -year -old kid can do quite a bit, you know, looking after little ones from babies on up to three and four and five years old. You knew enough to kind of keep them out of too much trouble.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she in bed for much of this time when you were home helping instead of at school?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, whenever, I was never out so much when she was able to be out of bed and so on. She wasn't in bed, well, maybe two or three weeks at a time, and maybe in another two or three weeks, if she wasn't ill, some of the children would be until they would be a need, you know. But it was just, maybe I'd get to school one week out of some months, and then some months maybe, I'd be there all but maybe only six or seven days out of the month.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But all in all, if they'd had all my record through the years that I went, I don't think I would have schooled more than, what would you say, more than half of the time?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Much more than half. Something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You can remember looking at the cards, I was talking about it, don't you?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well then, after you two got married, you had to do all that work over again at home. I mean, it was all that work you had to do just the same.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: After I got married, I was working for us, started having my own kids. And then when I got a chance to go out in the field some, because I liked to go out, you know, when he'd be mowing and he'd let me drive a team and rake, and I went not because he ordered me to or anything, but just simply that I liked to, that I wanted to be out. And then up at Teak, when we lived there, there was one summer that, oh, there was a neighbor had a job and he had quite a bit of hay to put up. And he just kept trying to find somebody to put it up, you know, so he could stay on the job. And so Ed didn't have anybody to help him and our kids were small. So he took the job of hauling the hay, the wagon and the team, and I went with him and helped him pitch it on, you know, hauled out of the shock.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mowed it? Did you mow it? Or just haul it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, I don't think so. I think I just hauled it. I think I just hauled it and put it in.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And anyway, I put us two or four days or so to put the hay in. And we'd get our chores done and take the little kids and drive out what was about three miles into this place. And then we'd haul hay all day. And I can remember we needed the money so bad and I think it was $30 we got for putting that hay in. And I'd help pitch on the wagon and then mow it back in the barn and things like that. So I was his hay hand through that job and we were needing the money and very glad to get it. And I didn't think really too much about it. It was something that I kind of enjoyed doing, you know.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: It sounds like you really had rough times, I mean, you know, to where you just really needed the money that you could get. Well, we never have had very much wealth financially. I had quite a little sickness after I was married. I have lots of tonsillitis. So we let it go too long really before we had tonsils taken out. And I kind of ruined my health. After our second child was born, I was a whole year before I was getting straightened out to where I could do very much work. And that made it pretty rough on him, you know, and he had to help with the house and sometimes we could have somebody with us part of the time. But, oh, look back on it, it seemed kind of rough at the time and now I, it seemed like you kind of grew from it more than you would from having, you know, just everything handy and just at your fingertips. Like our three older children, you know, had kind of rough and had to walk quite a ways to school and the things that they used to have much. And, well, if you didn't have much, well, they had much better opportunity and much better chance than the older ones did, you know, finances and things got a little bit better. And, but the older ones feel that they really, when they look around at their own kids and their grandkids and all, and they talk about it and they feel that they really, you know, didn't miss anything because they felt that they learned a lot about life too, you know, and how to consider others. And, I know that whenever they got a treat of any kind, and it's basically the oldest one, why, she got a, Nicola got a candy bar somebody gave it to and she never ate it or anything until she got home and cut it out equally and divided it with the others and so on. So, nowadays why kids, I don't know, they're not all that way, I don't mean that, but I can see it in the little younger ones when they're running into the store or something all the time or maybe they'll give somebody a bite. Once in a while they'll divvy up, but it seemed like it's just different somehow, I can see it in our grandkids, they're different than other kids. And there was one thing that, there wasn't too much to try to keep up with Joneses because pretty near everybody were in the same boat when we were, you know, the neighbors and the neighborhoods we lived in. There wasn't very many families that was enough ahead of you, you know, or what you consider real well to do, but you need to feel too out of place. Most everybody was just about the same boat, willing to share and so on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much did the kids, did they really pull together as they were growing up? I mean, did the family work as a, operate as a unit, you know what I mean? Or were they kind of, I mean, were they sharing in your concerns about, you know, having enough to make do?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Or did they, were they, yeah, when they were, yeah.

speaker: Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh, yeah, we never, I didn't think we ever had in trouble, did you?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No. We got along really quite good, we've been pretty proud of the kids in that respect. And I know our oldest boy, one time I was talking to him and I said, well the two younger ones, while they didn't have anything and everything, why, he was working pretty steady then and they'd had better opportunities and both of them went into good jobs right out of high school. And so, I was talking to Virgil about how it was with them and how lucky they were, you know, if they had a treat or if they hadn't been. And the things that we did and had and didn't have, oh well, he says, well, Ma, we didn't miss it. And he said, well, you just never missed it, you don't, just don't know the difference, you don't have everything now.

speaker: Dixie Groseclose
words: I said, you don't miss it, do you?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And it's just been, well, I got thinking about it and you just knew you wasn't going to have it and really, you didn't spend your time grieving over it, you just made, just made do. I was always laughing about we're the making do family, I said, if we didn't have it, we could do it out or find something else to take its place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there ever times, Ed, when you were worried about having enough to make do?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, I don't know what I worried much about, I just made do with what I had.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: There's times that he had it awful rough and, oh, isn't it, and he is a type of person.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I just had to take it as what I got and make do with that. There wasn't nothing else I, wasn't nothing to worry about because... I guess he's... Well, in the 30s there, there wasn't nobody, there was nothing to do, there was five or six people in our neighborhood down there that needed work. But there was no use to go anywhere else because when you went over in the cities, well, it was there by the hundreds that was looking for work. So... Well, I just had, we had a little place we could grow gardens and grow most of the stuff we eat, so we got much better off than most people. We had some chickens and generally a pig or two and a cow or so. Well, I got down once, I didn't have any cows. I had, and then I got a hold of two and the train killed them both one day. They got on the track, train killed them both, and then the company, they was kind of slow about paying. I got 90, I got 90 dollars for the two of them at that time.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: That bought us another cow.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, everybody else wanted more for their cow though than what the railroad paid me for them. But at least we got something back out of it. They was so slow, I told them when they didn't pay me, I was going to put a milk, 15 dollar a month milk bill on and add it on because that was all the milk cows I had. And then they paid me. Oh, when you got an animal killed by the railroad, you was generally about six months of getting a payer or longer. My folks' places down there was railroad run right through them. They'd put the cattle on one side of the aisle on the other and then they'd come and crawl through the fence. Well, there was always good feed on the railroad. The stuff would get the pushing on that fence. Some would learn how to crawl through and others would just naturally get through and then didn't know how to get back out. That was the ones that got killed.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I think we got about five. My brother got five killed at one time. And then he got about three horses killed and another crippled at one time on the track.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you find that you could get by by bartering and not using money in the early days? I mean, was there many chances for that?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, yes, there were a lot of that, you know, like... Well, you could trade words. Well, you didn't know how much it cost you to produce an egg. But then you could always take them to the store and trade them for a little something. They generally, oh, eggs run from 15 to 25 cents here in this country. But back in the East, they used to go to five cents a dozen back there.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: That was about 1904.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Nineteen one and a long that. We left the East in two. Got out here where eggs were two bits that time.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I can't remember exactly how much eggs was when I come out, you know, in 1907. But I can remember they bought what they called for kale a lot of time now. You know, they call it calico and make clothes. You could get it for five, ten cents a yard. And you can get the nicest lace about they had for five cents a yard. I like that. Well, my mother worked a lot for 70 cents a week. I managed to do pretty well on it. Then finally, I believe the most she ever got was about two and a half a week. She worked for a doctor. His family.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: That was before she come west. She never worked for no wages after she came out here. No, after she came out in 1908.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She had two and a half a week?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, back in 1900, why, girls would go and work for 50 cents a week.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And a woman, a young woman would go and do all the housework for a dollar a week. Back in them good old days.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Instead of five and eight and ten dollars a day. Finally.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: But of course, you could buy an awful lot without a lot of money then, too. Well, back at that time there, what they call that calico was three cents a yard. Oh, gee, for about twelve cents they could buy enough of calico to make a big dress or two dresses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you always have enough clothes for the kids? Or did you find you had to mend them and patch them and make them?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I guess there are when I was growing up. Well, both. Well, there was a lot of making over and hand -me -downs. People give things to my mother. Being good at sewing, she did an awful lot of making over out of older things. And Dad was well acquainted with a lot of railroad men. You know, like conductors on trains or engineers and all like that. A lot of trains went through. And he was a cripple man, too. So, instead of sending things to the Goodwill, or I never heard of the Goodwill then, you know, really. But they would sometimes throw off sacks, gunny sacks. Of clothes, older suits and dresses and whatnot and discards. Why they knew the family didn't have very much. They'd throw that off and my mother would rip up the suits. She made the boys, you know, suits. I've got pictures of them over there. All dressed up and clothes she made. And she made, you know, their trousers and shirts. So, once in a while it was made out of new material. But we didn't get anything much already made that was bought. And then with us, well, I didn't like to sew very well. I have did quite a bit of it. We used a lot of making over and quite a lot of mending and all like that, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did your stepfather do to make a living?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, he trapped a lot during the winter. At one time, that was before he was married, you know, he had a little store. And then...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had an arrow?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: He raised, you know, corn and hay and had cows and chickens. He raised hogs for meat. Then he'd go out... There was quite a little business out there at one time. He would work out in the harvest fields some during the harvest period. And then in the wintertime, why, he'd make him and Ed's brother Will work together a lot. And they'd reasonably good with the trapping.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, at that time, the skunks, there was a Dr. Foster here in Judetta. And he was a rubbing doctor and he would take skunk oil and he'd give a dollar a pint for skunk oil. And he'd take that and rub his patients with it for arthritis or rheumatism, stuff like that. He used that skunk oil, so they sold... He would handle all the skunk oil they'd get. There wasn't too many people catching them and rendering that out. And then there was a Chinaman. He'd run on to down there in Lewiston and would give twenty -five cents for skunk gall. So, well, the skunks he'd get, he'd get twenty -five cents for the gall and a dollar a pint for the oil. I don't think a skunk possibly would make a pint of oil, but then they took a lot of skunks to make.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about skin? What about the fur?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, the fur, they got... I don't know just what they got for that, but they got something out of the fur, too. And then that oil and the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they trap anything else, Ed? Huh? Did they trap anything else?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh, yes, they trapped... Well, at that time, coons were just about gone out of the country, but... Coyotes, they picked up quite a few of them. You know, in them days, anyway, coyotes had a bounty on them, two and a half, and then they'd get the fur out of them in the wintertime. So, yeah, well, an old coyote made a fella quite a little money, but...

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Nowadays, they don't let you... they won't let you kill a coyote, hardly.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: You figured that the Indians got lice off the whites if they got it from any place.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah.

speaker: Dixie Groseclose
words: That's true?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, yes. Well, now, that hobo that I was telling you about stayed with us there then, and before we come to the other... Come out here, my brothers were staying... batching in with a fella up the little potlice there, and there's a white man coming. Stayed with them. He come there with some Indians. And then he got these white fellas, stayed there a day or two, and he give them lice, so... Chances are he'd give the Indians lice before he come there.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And then... Stayed there, and they buried my brother's horse. Well, one brother had a saddle, and the other had a horse, and they put the two together, and this fella buried them. To go somewhere, and he never did come back. Nice guy.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: These are the four oldest brothers, and here they are when they're a little older, but all the clothes that they're on, you know, were made... These suits were made out of hand -me -downs and suits and things. And then these, they just barefooted them all.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: They said the man... Oh, he was burning a brush, and the man was working on Sunday, and he's burning brush, and he'd do his piece, and then the fella... Well, I don't know what, some fella told him that he'd send him where there wasn't no Sunday, so he sent him to the moon. And that's where the man, that's the way the man got on the moon. He was burning brush on Sunday.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: That's the old story. That old, old story about Solomon Grundy burning brush on Sunday. It was kind of a... what you call it.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: That was an old story. That's the way the man got on the moon. It was a burner working on Sunday. He was burning brush on Sunday. Well, that's the old table.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, you know, this, you sang in here, Ed, too, which you said to me before, Ed, you think the Indian was more civilized than the white man. And I'm just curious about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You really think it's true?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well... I'm not saying no. I'm not saying I disagree with you a bit. When the white man come here, the things that they done, well, now the first ones would come here, the first settlement was a bunch of outlaws. You know, they were, said they were a bunch of gamblers that had been broke and they come here to find money, find gold. That's what they come for.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Come where?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Come to America. And there was only one man in the bunch that knowed enough to govern them and that was John Smith. Well, he went to the Indians and they gave him corn. The first time he got corn for a little, I don't know, maybe a few beads. He went back again and they were slow about giving him corn. He took a gun and went to the chief and told him that he'd fill up their ship with corn or he'd fill them with their dead bodies. And took the corn away from them. Held them up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think of that today if a man come to your place and stranger and you treat him good and he went away and come back a little while later and held you up and took everything you had?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: That's the kind of fellows the first settlers was in this country. Now then the Indians... No, but they didn't take it all when they forced them to give them what they wanted. Yeah, they took what they wanted from them by force. And then they trade them a little something and then the Frenchmen, they come and went up to Mississippi and took that country there. Everything in Mississippi, that was theirs. Well then they sold that to the United States, give them twenty million dollars for it. And then they told all over the world, come out this free country here, come in here and they come and went to take in Indian land. The Siouxs. Some of the Indians, there might have been a few of them that worshiped idols. But the Siouxs and the Nesperces, they wasn't that kind. The Siouxs, they had a history, I read part of it of theirs, that they believed in divine healing. The great medicine, they called them the great medicine. And they were very proud of their women. The women was the mothers of men. And anybody that wronged a woman, a Sioux woman, why, he was in trouble. If one wronged them and didn't take them and take care of them, they'd take them over the hill and kill them with rocks. You told me about that. Well then these three drunken cowboys come and caught these two squads and kept them overnight. And then turned them out and the next night three white men were hung. Well them men had no business going into that Sioux country. That was a nation of different people. But of course they weren't as strong, they weren't as well fixed militarily as the white men. So he just, well that was the way in them days. If one bunch of people wasn't as well prepared with the armies as the other one, well the other one just run over them. That wasn't considered wrong in them days.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: The Indians were pretty civilized when we found them all.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, they had awful good morals. The old, old, uh, George's father, he says the world don't belong to anybody. He says you can use the world as long as you're here and then it blows to somebody else. That was his theory. He was one of Spalding's first converts. Well the world belongs to God and we're just planets. Well you're here as long as you're here and what you do with it. But then the Indian was here and he had a good living. The animals and stuff that growed in this country here was, he made a good living. Got along fine here. And he had no idea, that's another funny thing, he had no idea of any other part of the world except where he was. Well the other people over there had no idea there was any other part of the world except what they had. They knowed of India but they couldn't get there because the Turks blocked the trail. And Columbus got the idea he could come around this way and get to India. And he come to America, well America didn't, the Indians didn't protect their country because nobody else, there was no other part of the world to them but that. So the white man come and they treated him good and he got a foothold and he kept kicking the Indians around.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Just taking a little more. Taking a little more all the time until he took the whole country over. Then the ones with this nest first that didn't want boos on their reservation. But the white man come in here and bootleggers sold it to them. And they told the death of an awful lot of good Indians by peddling boos to them.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: The young people wouldn't agree so much. He's quite tolerant toward the Indians because they were here first. But these younger people now where the Indians are finally getting money back from all of this and doing right, they don't think the Indians have got it coming.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well there's a lot of people that don't think they've got it coming and there's a lot of them that do think they've got it coming. But then in the early days the people didn't think, well an Indian was nothing, just kick him out, shoot him, do anything with him. And a nigger the same way. I'll tell you a story about a policeman my brother knew back in old Virginia along about 1900 and three, four or so years ago. And down there this policeman shot a nigger. Well the nigger was two bits a dozen and you know, kill a nigger you would figure nothing. But it got on this policeman's mind. And when he'd go to bed at night this nigger would be getting in bed with him. And when he'd belt he'd see the nigger walking with him. He shot himself. But that was up to that time you know. It wasn't considered nothing but after he'd killed that one, why he just got spooked by it and he went crazy, killed himself. So that's one thing you know, it's not, this thing killed a man. That's something that's not so good after you've done it, why most likely it'll get your mind. You might think, oh I'll get mad at him, I'll kill him. Well then if you just turn loose and never get caught, why it'll most likely show up on you sometime. And then there's a funny thing about people, gunmen, who shoot at a man. He's a great big thing. But awful good marksman how they can shoot at a man and miss him, as big a thing as he is. I don't know, there's just something about your nerve and things out of the way that... Maybe they don't always intend to hit you.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: That gun never snapped, it was a good gun. He pulled it out and he shot at a stump and it fired every shot just as regularly as they come. But when he pulled it on that man it snapped. Every bullet around snapped. And then fire when he pulled it on that man, but going home, pulled it on a stump, it fired every shot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He fired more than once at this man he got mad at?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: He never fired the gun, he snapped it clear round at him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why'd he snap it clear round?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, he was a six shooter and he tried to shoot him six times and the gun snapped every time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say snap, what does that mean?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Just snap, it don't fire. Clicked but didn't fire. Yes, clicked but didn't fire. But when he took it and cut it with that stump, it fired every time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He must have been pretty mad at that guy, he was ready to kill him, was he?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, he was drunk and he was killing. It wasn't his fault at the time he didn't kill him. No, it wasn't his fault, it was just the almighty had his arms around him that kept that gun from firing.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, you know, you grew up around the Indians out here. Yeah, I grew up among them here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of their way of dealing with people as compared to the white man's way?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, they were honest. Well, if there was honest ones then they were just like that. Well, there were some of them that would be crooked, but they learned mostly crookedness from the white man. They were honorable people. When they said you do something, they'd carry it out. Sometimes they'd pull some funny tricks. Like my sister at homestead down there, they'd had the line surveyed between her and the Indians. And they set up some stakes there, one tall stakes and some low ones. And the line at the fence had never been through. The Indian fence come way down around on her place. So this morning my brother got up and went out on the porch and there this old lady, Allen, and an Indian neighbor, Jim Red Coyote, was out there fooling around the corner. And Jim had the stake, the big stake. But Allen put it back and he took over towards the railroad track. My brother Grover says, well maybe he just wanted to go down and track the site, but when he got over on the railroad, he didn't stop on there. There was a big fill and he got behind the railroad track and went up to the other Indian's place up there. Well, they decided that would be a bad day as any to put the fence through. So my brother went up to his corner, 40 rods away, and come down and sighted and looked at where this corner was, where they'd had there in the flat. And when he looked at that and then looked on over across the country, well, he wasn't very fertile, he was a quarter of a mile off. The line was supposed to have went this way. And from where they'd moved that corner there, a rod or two in the field, it was way off over this way by the time they got over the river. That wouldn't do. So they had to go up to the school section corner then on the hill a quarter of a mile up there, and come from there down to where they, from sight from there to the established corner, then they could get the line through and they got the line through and put the main part of the fence in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they put it back where it had been?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, we got back where it was by going up the school section corner a quarter of a mile further, and then looking down at my brother's corner where the fence was already in, but his corner post leaned a little. And after we got the fence in, the Indians had brigs to survey it, to come and survey it, and he moved the fence three feet out. We got up on the Indian three feet, and they put in the fence then the whole way through, and tore our fence out and threw it off of their place. So we never did. There was always the Indian fence the whole way along that line there. We never did put in in the fence. You know, you were talking about that. I want to ask you more about the Indians, but that reminds me of that story you told me about Kimberly and his fence being off, his line being off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did he get his line off in the first place?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, he just, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, had there been a survey before he was there, or what? Had the land been surveyed already, or what?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, he'd never had it surveyed. He got, must have started to the Indian Corner somewhere, and then just went out and measured too far.

speaker: Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, did he buy it, or?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: He homesteaded. Him and Tom Hall homesteaded both of them in that draw there. Tom was his son -in -law. And Kimberly, he just got in and got a place located there, and then just built a fence until he got tired of fencing. There was nobody around him, so he just stuck his fence way out, took in twenty, thirty rods more than what he had coming. So he just didn't pay no attention to it. No, he didn't pay no attention. He just took what he wanted. Built his house on vacant land and put an orchard on vacant land. He let a patch of good land there and up on his place, and the draw wasn't any good. So he put out his orchard and orchard on vacant land, built his house on vacant land, built his barn on vacant land, and proved up on it that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year was it when he left and your brother got his place?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I don't know this year.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I don't know what year he sold out to Fisher and left the country here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you still a kid?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah. Oh, it don't matter. There were seven, oh, just seven or eight along there that he got out of here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Among the Indians that lived around where you were raised, did you have any personal friends? Ones that you were close to?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, I had one. There was one boy that, he was a little older than I was, but Johnson Williams. He was, he went off to school and he learned to talk English. And he come back and he wouldn't talk Indian anymore. Only to an old Indian that couldn't understand English. So one like that he'd talk a little, but very little. And I used to ask him what the Indians called something. He didn't know. He would never tell me what anything was in Indian. If he'd been a man, a boy that would talk, told me what stuff was in Indian, I'd have learned to talk Indian. But no, he wouldn't do it. If I heard an Indian word and would tell him the word, he would interpret it, tell me what it was in English, but he wouldn't tell me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why not, you think? You just didn't want to speak it anymore?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Just didn't want to, just didn't want to speak it. And then he learned all his family. They all learned to speak when his brother, his little sister, went to school. They had learned to talk. They could speak English, you know, when they first started school. That was a great help to them. Now there's some other girls went, well there's Sam Waters down here. When he started school he couldn't talk a word of English. He had to learn it along with his schoolwork. And then the McCatty girls, Tilly and Jane, they had to learn English from reading in their books and talking here and the teacher talked. But the Williamses, they, they know how to talk when they went to school. They could talk good. Because Johnson, Johnson learned them. He only lived to be about 19 when he died.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: There's a little tombstone down there across from, across the river from Albright. And then there's a locust tree up there. I planted the locust tree and this tombstone, there's eight Indians epitaphs on that tombstone. The Williams family there, they, Will Johnson and... The mighty guy. There's tuberculosis. And Tom, and I don't know what his wife's name was. She never could talk, never could talk English or never would. But then Esther and Agnes, Tom and his wife and Johnson. And then there's three younger ones. They ate up more on that there. And then there was a stranger buried. There was nine in that graveyard over there. Where that tombstone is and that's one locust tree standing up there by it. I planted a locust tree there when I rented the place and used to farm it there for a while. Well I rent it because there's wood on it. I needed the wood. The driftwood, there's lots of driftwood on there and I rented the place for that. And then I did farm it a little. I would, well I'd rent it for the, I don't know, they paid $20 a year for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What'd you use the wood for?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: For my house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Firewood?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, I got the wood out of there for two, three years. Before I rented the place where Alec Williams lived down there, I rented that and was there for about two years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did your friend go to school when they said he went away?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: He went to school down there at the era, down there where Joe Wing lives. That's where the school house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's where he learned his English? Huh?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, I don't know where he went to learn his English. Some Indian school somewhere. I don't know whether the... His father and his family lived on that place and their house was kindly under the, under the hill over there on the first side of the river. Down in there, and I guess that was a damp place in that flat. Must have been what caused them all to have TB and die young.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: May I possibly live in that in there right close to the river?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: But Amos, an Indian could take, could split up a piece of ground, but a white man couldn't. If you had had forty acres, if there was five acres of good land on the rest of it all scabbed, you had to take the whole forty to get that. An Indian could take ten acres or five or twenty or whatever it was, so Amos split two forties and took all that ground in there that was rock over across the railroad. That was A -1 farmland when the railroad went in there. But they cut that bluff off and turned the river down in there and it just kept eating out of the way. So instead of going right down the railroad where it was supposed to, it went clear over against the other hill now, washed all that flat away. About twenty -five acres there. But them Indians, they moved the corners. And there's nobody today knows but them Indians where them corners were supposed to be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They moved the corners?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, they just destroyed the corners that were Briggs and Marks. We couldn't ever find them. Briggs should tell you close to where they was, but exactly where the corners was, nobody ever found them. They were supposed to be a rock mark. They were quarter corner. They got rid of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you trust when the Indians told you something, did you trust them, what they said?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, no, not too much.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: They would lie, but they wouldn't steal. Early day ones. They're different now.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I wanted to ask you about Foster and about when you had mentioned him to me just when I was leaving last time. I wanted you to tell me about when you went down there for treatment.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, it was just that he had become such an alcoholic at that time until you couldn't depend on him for the treatment. Ed had left me there at, they had, they called it the health home right by where they had the offices of different doctors, you know, for to do this. Osteopathic, I think that's what they were. Osteopathic, yeah. And so he said I get two treatments a day and the food that kind of diets, you know, I'm supposed to be on. So he thought, well, give me the best advantage, you know, by the time I was down there. So he left me there at the home. And to begin with, my old morning or two I got what I was supposed to have and then we got, so they put things out, you know, that didn't agree with me and it wasn't ordered by the doctor. And I had a little trouble there, you know, and he had to get after them to get them to fix the things I was supposed to have. And then I went along two or three days just fine. And then one time I went over to take my treatment and he was no condition to give me one. So I went on back over and waited till the time for the evening treatment and I went back and he's still, you know, kind of under the weather. So I was kind of a little disturbed and, what was it, a marshal, Dr. Marshall, Marsh, Dr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Marsh?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I'm not sure about his name now, Dr. Marsh I think. And he told me, he said, well, rather than for you to miss a treatment, he said, if you prefer, I will give you a treatment, you know. Well, I thought that was better than missing. So he gave me the treatment that night. Well, the next morning I went over and didn't see nothing of Dr. Foster at first. And then so Dr. Marsh came by and wanted to take another treatment, you know, by him. So I told him I did. So he was giving me a treatment when Dr. Foster walked in. And he still wasn't, you know, he was drinking enough that he wasn't too good. But he got kind of mad.

speaker: Dixie Groseclose
words: He said, oh, so you just took it on yourself to change doctors, have you?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And I said, well, the idea was, I said, you weren't available. And so I did want to miss my treatments. And so I don't know. I guess maybe Foster gave me a treatment or two after that. But he was always kind of disgruntled, you know, about it some way.

speaker: Dixie Groseclose
words: And then, what was it that happened to the cloudburst? What happened?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: The cloudburst come and tore the water system out. And they had an awful time of getting water around there. Yeah, and they hauled it and put it on the street corners in barrels, you know, for the people. And couldn't get good water. And so I ended up, he just took me home, you know.

speaker: Dixie Groseclose
words: Was this during the time that you were sick after having your baby?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, it was the year after the baby, the second child.

speaker: Dixie Groseclose
words: And how was she?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: About five months old when I was down there, Foster. I don't know about that. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Foster have a good reputation at that time?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, outside of his drinking. He had had a good reputation up to that time. I don't know much about him after that. But I know he got to drinking a lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that when he was up here in Juliaetta that he was highly regarded while he was here?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, he was here. Apparently, as far as we know, we had no personal contacts ourselves with him at that time. Well, my folks did.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: My uncle and him was great, great friends here. And he and his wife finally quit. And I happened to see her. She came up to see my aunt up there one time. And she was telling a story about the fellow coming and selling twelve, I believe twelve bottles of moon for thirty dollars. And he gives the fellow a sample. And she quit the doctor then. And she said, I think the old doctor was in on that. Got caught on that. But he took a lot of people for thirty dollars for twelve bottles of drinks, booze, supposed to be booze. And when they got to the cold tea, and he was gone, they figured he'd come from North Carolina and he got money enough that way to go back. So, I suppose he went back. Nobody knows what became of him, but he didn't show up.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, maybe some of his sons might be alive and still know, but he was really supposed to have cured several cancers of his breast. I don't know that internally or anything like that, that he could do anything for them. But the lumps that came and all. And they put what they called a burner on. I don't know. But I've seen the people that have had the treatment. And when they went on, they just walk practically day and night. Just out walking the street. You know, just walk, walk because of the pain. But there were several people that seemed, I don't know, of course following them up the years afterward when they never come back on them. So the treatment itself was kind of painful. Well, they have very much so. And then he had a niece that passed away with cancer of the breast. And she was expecting a little one. And she noticed just before it was born, she noticed this lump. But they thought, you know, it didn't make too much of a thought. It was milk land or something. So when the youngster was born, didn't go to the hospital them days, the doctor came to the home. And she said to her husband, she said, I mustn't forget to ask the doctor about this lump. And so she said that everything was overtaken care of. And the doctor started to leave. And he even got out to his car and she told her husband, well call him back. I never mentioned the lump.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he called him and he came back in and he said to her, he said, oh, so you're another one of these women that's got cancer, huh?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And just kind of laughed or said, I think they've got cancer. And then when he examined her, he said, well, you know, I am a little apprehensive about this. And he said, just as soon as you're able, you know, and they just stand dead about ten days those days, instead of getting up. So he said, just as soon as you're up, come into my office because I want to make further examination.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was foster?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, this wasn't foster. No, this wasn't foster yet. Yeah, ma 'am, indeed. It wasn't foster yet at that time. But she didn't go right in. By that time, I don't remember, just watch, she said something about it. Her husband told her she's kind of imaginative. She got kind of mad and she thought, oh well, he had a wife. She was the second or third wife and he had buried one woman with cancer. So she said, I just thought, well, if he feels that way, you know, she just got mad and kind of stubborn. So it went on, she didn't go in right away. It went on for a few weeks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Boy, was he mad at her because he was mad at her for?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, he wasn't mad at her, but he just made some remark about maybe she was imagining more or something and it made her sore. So when she went in, finally did go to the doctor. He said to her, he said, well, I can't be sure what this is until it's taken out and tested. But he said, I'm very concerned and he said, I would like to operate immediately. And she said, well, I don't know that I want this operation, you know, just that quick. She said, I would like to see if I could get in touch with foster. So she heard and knew him, you know, around this country and see if some of the boys or something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And she said, could I wait long enough or could I go and see them?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: He said, you go see them if you know where they are and all like that. But he says, whatever you do, don't put it off. He says, do it immediately. Well, by the time she rode up here to know where they was, by the time she got foster, one of the boys, the old man, was gone. She got him traced down when she found one of them. And he was in Portland, not far from Bevacombe, not far from where she was at. And when she got him traced down, it was about six weeks later. So when she got to him, he said, well, I've got to make a bunch of tests before I can be sure. So that was about three weeks before he got all his testing done. And then he told her, it definitely is cancer. And he said, I could put a burner on and take it off if it hadn't gone into your glands. But he said, it's gone in already, gone into your glands. And he said, you're never in the world to withstand the pain that you'd have to endure. But he said, if they'd gotten in touch immediately right at first, he thought he could have did something for her. She lasted a year.

speaker: Dixie Groseclose
words: She left, what, four? Five? Five kids?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Five kids. And her baby was a year old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it was too late to have any operation by that point?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: It was too late by that time for the surgery to do anything because, and then by the time she died, of course it had gone all over. She was only 40 years old.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh my.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: You know, speaking about Foster, there was a story that I heard that kids, and all people around Juliaetta, used to think that they would see after Foster moved down to Clarkson from here, looking at the house, they would see a figure of, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What have you heard about that?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, I've heard that, we've heard about that. That was a haunted house. That was a nurse.

speaker: Dixie Groseclose
words: Wasn't it supposed to be kind of a nurse that would come out on the porch up there that they would see?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I don't know what is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why don't they see it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I think it's supposed to be seen from Macy Nye's house up here, up to the house up there now where, where Miller lives, Floyd Miller.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, but I haven't heard anything about it in a later year. Oh no, I haven't heard anything. Well, I'm talking about what the story was back then. Now, I've heard a couple different stories, but I don't know which might be the one that people told, but I heard one, there was a nurse about who had killed a baby or something up there, and she was standing in the window.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Another story I heard was that it was Foster's wife, a figure of her standing in the window up there.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I don't know, I only heard that there'd be something come out kind of at the door or kind of on the porch or something that looked kind of like a nurse that would appear, you know, at different times. It's the only one that I have heard. I never fit much stock in, is what I mean. But yeah, I've heard it, you know.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I've heard that, but I don't know how to let ghosts stuff like that out. I wouldn't believe there's such thing if I'd seen it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear ghost stories back in the south?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh yes, I've heard them all, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which stories? Would you tell me a little bit of what you heard from back there?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I'm not saying if you believe in it or not, I'm just wondering what you heard. Well, the thing that, the remedies for being bewitched. Now if you had your cream, if your cream wouldn't turn, it was bewitched. So the thing to do to get that cream to turn was to take a sickle, one of these little hand sickles, take that and heat it red hot and put that down your milk and that would kill the ghost and you'd turn your milk would turn. That was a way to get your milk to turn. I think people were crying that quite a few of them were awful superstitious back then. Oh yeah, there was back in them days lots of superstitious people. Kind of half way bleeding. That was the, with that. And then it was bad luck to sweep down a spider web, a cobweb we call them, big cobweb up in the house. You broke one of them to keep from having bad luck. You were supposed to take so many yards of yarn that you'd blow, you know, half a yard and take that there and stand in front of the fireplace and throw it over your left shoulder into the fire and burn that yarn to stop the bad luck for...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: From the web?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: From breaking that spider web, yeah. Now I had some relatives that... That was a big ghost story. It was her uncle.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: He believed in witches so his ox was sick. He went to the witch doctor, they had one back there, and went to him to find out what to do for his ox. Well during the time the old man was gone the ox got sick and the boys put him in the barn. He got in the corn and the boys put him in the barn. The witch doctor told the old man to come home and draw the picture of an ugly man on a barn door. And then get up before sun up in the morning and not say anything to anybody and shoot that picture with a silver bullet. That would break the spell on the ox. Well the boys put the ox in there and he heard the old man a -coming and he come with his head up to the door to be let out to pasture. And the old man he shot the... Shot the ugly picture on his door and he heard a racket in the barn and went in and went and looked. He shot the old ox out between the eyes and killed him dead.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And then there was a woman back there that fought with a witch, all those beavers.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: They called the ox and said she was a witch. Jim Devers, her husband, her husband was a wizard too. There was one young fellow, one of them, one of them teaching how to be a wizard. Yeah, he was teaching but he said the first thing you got to do when you go to be a wizard you got to sell yourself to the devil. That young fellow, he didn't want to do that. So Doc Young, he believed that Jim was a witch or wizard. And my dad and old Jim Devers, they used to get together and make up stories to tell young. Well, my dad, they fixed it up, they told old Doc Young that he would go up to Totten's Branch, the little creek you had back there called Totten's Branch. He'd go up to Totten's Branch and many a time with his gun and never see a thing to shoot at. Well, he thought old Jim would boost him out of the reason of it. Well, anybody that goes out hunting, you may go for a month and not see a thing.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: That fox would be coming this way and a certain place would turn right back and go the other way. Well, it did. But that was the end of the ridge. It would come down one side of the ridge and it would go right back up the other side of the ridge. It didn't keep going straight. Then, old John, he went out and shot a fox, shot at one. Jim Devers took Grand College that same day. He shot at that fox with a silver bullet. He says, God, you know, I almost killed old Jim. He's the old Jim of the fox, you know. She thought he'd hit him and that's what caused him to have that bellyache.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, why did they think she was a witch? This was his wife?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, he was a wife. Oh, I don't know.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I guess a lot of things she told kind of halfway happened.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: She's a kind of peculiar old woman. They'd be scared to death.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They'd be scared to death by what she said?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
