words: Yeah. But I can remember the kid then, even after coming out here where the Virginia folks was, Liz Devers, Liz Devers, all Liz Devers, and they always called her the witch. But that's for ghosts, so I don't remember seeing really, or I sure don't talk much about ghosts.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: My brother, he tried to scare some people. One time, that ghost stuff, he sneaked up on top of their house and took a rope and tied it around a brick and dropped it down the chimney, you know, and he had a bump and a scraping around in there. They all ran outdoors and they got outside before he got his rope out. He done had enough to get his rope out and get off in there, but they got out before he got off in the house.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: It's funny, I never heard of a wizard before. I mean, I've heard the name, word wizard, but I've heard a lot about witches, but I didn't know that they had husbands that were wizards. Well, I'm thinking the witch and the wizard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That would be the husband and the wife, huh?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, you don't have to be a wife, but then he's a man, he's a man -witch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A wizard, just a man -witch? Do you think he thought he was or do you think he never thought he was?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I don't think so, I don't know. They just got that. Well, you know, they used to burn people. The English used to burn people, witches.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That there, what was the name of that great general that led the French?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Joan of Arc?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Joan of Arc, you know, they burnt her for a witch. And then over in the colonies, I think they burnt some witches there. Yeah, oh yeah.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Now that's something the Indians, they were terrible people. They burnt people at the stake, but there was our white man that burnt witches. Take somebody they didn't like and thought they'd done queer things and they'd burn them. They were just as bad as the Indians.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Indians.

speaker: Dixie Groseclose
words: Have you ever heard anything about these devil worshippers?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: They were so scary and near and far about them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this recent, recently or long? A year or two ago, some of them said, where was it up toward?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: They got the butcher and cattle and just taken some of the end of the sides out of them.

speaker: Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, wasn't it up toward Winchester or someplace?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Some of them strung up across the road and trying to stop a car. Oh yeah. I know that they were scared enough about it that a lot of people said that, well, Doug John, the one up here, he got real disturbed about them, you know. And he told his mother, he said, if you ever go to Lewiston or drive somewhere and you see a bunch crossing the road holding hands, you know, they just come up over a bank or just walk across the road and hold hands and just stand there for the car to come. Well, he said, just pour on the gas and go right on. He said, if you hit one, why, that's alright, if you're still able to go, then they'll leave. If you kill one of them, they say then they'll skin that one out, the person out, and they'll go on. But otherwise they might take you, you know, if they get you stopped or anything, because it's a sacrifice or something that they do. But they said if you kill one of their own, you know, just that way, then they'll go on to another place. But a year or two ago, it was quite a bit sad. And there was supposed to have been, it was up near Coeur d 'Alene. I think it was that Virgil told me that it was supposed to really have been some other place. I've heard that, and Coeur d 'Alene was a place where that was going on. And then there was once, if there was some up toward Winchester at one time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was going to ask you, were there things about like signs for getting married, how you would know who you might marry?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: You know, I've heard that sort of thing. The house, why, that meant somebody was going to die or it fluttered in your window. Oh yeah, that was a woman, a girl I had up there, Teekin. It was superstitious.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I had her coming and work for me a few days. And the dog a -howling was a sign of bad luck or death or something that way. And if a bird come and fluttered in your window, well, I had an apple tree.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Bring a hoe in the house?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, usually I always say bad luck, bring a hoe in the house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had an apple tree?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Had an apple tree set out there a ways, and the blue bird was getting that apple tree and you could see its reflection in the window. Boy, it would come in right up there and we would just have a great time there in that window. It would get tired and it would go back to the tree a while. Sit there a while and you'd see that reflection again and you'd come back. Boy, that was a sign of death.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: She told my wife that stuff and all that. I'll tell you, if I'd have known she had them foolish ideas in her head, I sure wouldn't have hired her on the place. But they were superstitious.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did she come from?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: They were Norwegians.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: They happened to live up there. It was a fine family. She was a lovely girl and also a nice person, but very superstitious. They had a crippled sister. Something happened and the sister was crippled. I can't remember now just what they did. She jumped off of a hat and they turned the wagon over.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: She was supposed to be an old man. He was a Norwegian and he talked off of broken too. He was a preacher. I don't know how I ever got him by understanding him when he preached, but he'd quit preaching. This girl's name was Ruth and he called her Root. She turned the wagon over and said, I jumped and Root jumped and Root didn't jump far enough.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: She got hurt bad, didn't she, turning the wagon over. I can't remember what the reason was. They thought all this happened, but they had some superstition.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, about why it happened.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So Lillian, wasn't that her name?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah. She would tell me about that, you know, because that was the cause of Ruth. I can't just remember why, some superstition. When that burden came, she'd say, well, she didn't like it. Of course, he'd be out at work. He couldn't stay at the house all the time. The kids was little and there I was, sick. So, of course, I figured that my folks down here had smallpox pretty bad at the time. And every time after she'd get through with all her worries and our telephone would ring, I was scared for anybody to answer. I figured it'd be some of my folks was gone or else I was going to die or something because they had me in bed with an ice pack on my heart there for three weeks. I don't know if it was necessary enough. And then the dog howled there. They took me to the hospital. I wasn't doing very good. So the doctor decided they should take me to the hospital. Took me in the night. Then the doctor up there went by his brothers and left the oldest youngster. He was about three. And I had the baby with us, Peggy, second in line with the baby. And Louise was getting ready to leave. Of course, everybody started out. The dog pawed Louise. Just a howling and a howling. I was scared. I thought, well, that's the last time I'd ever see that place. But now then I could see how silly it was. He was just upset because everybody was gone and he couldn't go along. I don't suppose I'd leave anything if it was true anymore. But there while I was sick and kind of upset anyway, many things kind of happening.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I can almost be super stupid. She got down to weighing 115 pounds that time.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I still weigh 180 now, but 115 was pretty thin.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: So in other words, you two didn't really put much stock in superstitions yourselves. No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you court for very long before you got married? Did you court?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh, I guess I'd call it that way.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: She was just... I was a kid, me either. Well, I was 20 years old and I bought me a top buggy. My cousin was going to go away. She was 14, so she did. So she went up there to my cousin's. My cousin told me she wanted me to take her buggy riding before she went away. She was going just in two or three days. So Sunday, my brother's wife was down there and I had to take the top rig up to take the whole family up to Sunday school. Coming back then, I had to get around to get my cousin a buggy ride. So my cousin Joe and his sister and Dixie, they come down their rig to our place. I hung a hook from the hack and hooked her onto the buggy. The four of us came on down and brought her down home. My cousin, he was a statter. He said, He says, you're a darn fool if you don't take Dixie up to Sunday school next Sunday. Well, I was 20 years old, big bashful guy, and then I heard a little kid die, just a kid die. 11 years older than I. I said, well, I've got my consent.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, how is it with you?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: She'd be all right. So next Sunday, I come and got her. Took her up. They had a picnic that Sunday. Next Sunday, I took her and then I left the country up in the hills for quite a while. Somehow or other, something came up and we went together a few times again.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And he had another girl for a while and then he thought, well, she'd be quiet.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I don't know. Things just seemed to happen. I got acquainted with the school movement. I was going to have a big time with that winter and I got all crippled up. I couldn't do that. So as far as I could get was just when I got too tired of staying home, was to walk a mile over to Wilson's where she was and be there.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Everything just sort of started together that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you get crippled up?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Turned the wagon over. A load of poles went up through him and... Me and my cousin was all coming down the hill with a load of poles to build a corral. One place down there in Catholic else. We were coming on top of a little grave up here between here and Kendrick. And this fellow met us there with a car before our team and he had to back up to get out of the way. And every time he tried to back up, he'd kill the engine. That was better than six years ago, you know. Finally after about ten minutes there in the lights, that car I took my lead team off and pulled him out of the way and then put him back on. We come down the grave. Got just put near the foot of the grave and went over the grave and just turned the wagon upside down. Caught my cousin under it. I might have been knocked out for a while. I don't know. But when I come to, I was clear minded. So I went up to my cousin and I found him under that load of poles. There was a big rock there. And he was squeezed until he couldn't holler but there wasn't a bone broke in him. That rock held the load. Held the load up, you know, so it didn't break a bone in him. Didn't hurt him any. Well, he was numb when we got him out of there. So I got on, my wheel team was laying down. They just drove down the road and laying down and the leaders were up in the road. I went up and got on one of them. Started off the other so I took the lines off and unhooked them when I was on and just turned the other end loose and rode with the e -nurse. Then I went down the road hollering for help and this fella over there across the river where they've got that farm now. He answered me and I told him. So he folded around and a little hollers five men there to help. Well that load of poles was tightened with a spring pole up over a pole in old French Falls there. And then we put that pole in that there long pole. I had two of them on there. And that was turned up and them was under the bottom so you know them chains were tight. No way of getting them loose. I had a knife with a plier on it but my thumb here was numb. I couldn't use that. I gave it to somebody else but they couldn't use it. They cut a wire to link it in there but the other chain was good.

speaker: Edward Groseclose
words: So finally I says anybody around here got an axe?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Here did a tight chain you could chop it with an axe and that was the only show. Another fella says old men. Leland's got an axe and another one says I'll go get it. And he went up in the road and walked around three heads of horses and walked down there three quarters of a mile. About three quarters down and back to Leland's. Went a foot and got the axe. Another fella come and brought my other horse back to the one there. One fella said he'd send him to go by and he'd scare the folks if he went home lucky to go by when he passed him. Another one come and brought him back up. I said he's eleven miles from home. He said he'd be there pretty soon if he kept going away past me. They brought the horse back. They got up there and that chain with that axe and just one lick a piece and them chains just flew. So then they got the doctor. He come up in the car and brought us down here to this place over here. My uncle was there. We stayed overnight. Well, a fella rode down hill and my cousin up. Coming down here and we got down here and he says I can walk. Me and this guy looked at each other and grinned. He just hopped out of the car into the house and went. Well I started to get out and I'd been going up there and around just doing as much as anybody. It was perfectly clear of mind. I told him to get the axe to chop the chains and perfectly everything was done. I told him what to do. So yeah, it was perfectly clear of mind but I rode in that car down from up there to here. When I started to get out of that car somebody split me from my shoulder blade to the hip bone with an old dull knife. It wouldn't have hurt a bit worse. Then a fella he come and tried to help me. Boy, that was worse yet. There's no way for me to get out of there. Only wiggle out on my own. I got out and got up to the house. It was two weeks that I couldn't pick up a toothpick with this hand here. There wasn't nothing broke but just ligaments all just busted up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it all in your hand? Is it all in your hand and your arm?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: My hand here a little way and then my back. I threw him out and a bunch of calls. I lost three months.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: He sat on the high chair when he could kind of walk but he couldn't sit and get up. When he'd come to visit me, if there was the highest chair of the kids' high chair, that's what he'd sit on. He'd just bend. But he was crippled up for quite a while. You were really young at that point when you were going to school. Yeah, it seemed like an awful difference. It don't seem bad now but when we were going to school, see when I was eight, he was 15 and there were some 15, 16 -year -old girls and he was having a big time with them. Here I was a little, until he had the braids running around there.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: A lot of little funny things like that happened. And fellas don't know why but they fall for them little girls.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Then one time it was raining and they couldn't be outside. The big kids, of course, would get together and get them some books or be talking or something. Little kids would hang around to see what. They had a book of riddles and rhymes that they was giggling and reading. There was another grown boy or two, two of the girls and so on. They was reading and there was, I was pretty timid then. I didn't do much talking, kind of bashful and I was standing there listening at them. They read over the needles and pins, needles and pins, when a man marries his troubles again. He's laughed about that so much, you know, I didn't even remember it.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well Bill's kids are pictured as kids a lot more than what I did. She could never get married then. I just looked at the boy and he just never got married.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I guess I want him to wait for me but if it seems funny I'll think about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wait a minute, you played just needles and pins, how did that work?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: A little verse he had in the books, needles and pins, needles and pins.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you figure it meant you?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well that was her, she told me, she said you'd better never get married then. No, we were just a bunch of little kids hanging around there. I was always back, would never talk much and that's what they all noticed when they read that. So I just looked up, better never get married then. It just been a little funny after everybody.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well did you feel that you were, now how old were you when you two got married?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well I was almost seventeen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were almost seventeen?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I was married the 24th of December and I was seventeen and the 25th of February. So I married young.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you feel at all the time that you were real young to get married? Or did you feel like you were ready for marriage?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well I don't know, I suppose I was young if I'd been, I think that if I could start out all over again, I'd marry the same guy if you'd have me. But at the same time I might want to be a little bit older, might be I could have managed a little better or something, I don't know.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well she was the oldest one in the family and she got to going out to old parties and around like that. And her stepdad, he didn't think she should go so much. Him and her mother got to having quite a little trouble over her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your mother stuck up for you? Your mother stuck up for you?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: My mama always did, you know. A stepdad, kind of funny, they too never had any reason to distrust me. I'd always get home when they told me and everything. But I let a bunch of come in and the young kids or not and he'd sit and tell about all the times he went to the parties and had a bottle of whiskey and they'd get some old maids drunk and all these things, you know, and all the big times. And then if I went out just with the neighbors, to a party in the neighborhood, by then it was all wrong. I was always pretty good. Mama always played ball with me, you know, if I'd listen unless she'd let me go. But I'd have to be home maybe by 12 or 10 or 11. I don't know, might have been a time or two I didn't get to go, but most of the time I'd go. But a lot of the time, I had a very good friend and she had an uncle that was quite a lot older than any of us. And he played the banjo, he'd take his banjo and go play. We'd just go around the neighborhood to the different houses, you know. So he would come with Harriet and they'd get me a lot of times, you know, so I could go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would it be dances? Is that what it would be? Well, once in a while some of the two -step were waltz a little bit and they'd have the, what do you call them, what do they call them? Dance?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Square dances. Cadrill, square dance. Square dance some. And a lot of times just games or taffy pull, you know, and they'd make taffy. They had a lot of fun taffy pulls. And every party they had a taffy pull.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of games?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh, like, I have four of us, you know, and if you didn't answer, do... Spin the platter, I don't know what kind, quite a lot of... Playing out the windows and playing post office and all them kissing games.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: There was one family come in there one time, but they had a lot of fun the winter that they run the parties there. They had the most kissing games. If the people don't think nothing about it, well then some people, oh, some girls, we went one place to have one and some of the girls wouldn't go and they'd make this fellow mad. They'd say, if they was out in the dark they'd kiss, but they'd just have to play a game, you know. Oh, no, they wouldn't do nothing like that. But they had some, had some funny games. One of them was kiss both sides of the watch. Take a man's watch, you know, with a chain, put a girl and a boy up there and they'd have to kiss both sides of that watch. Hang it right down over their mouths and they were supposed to kiss both sides of that watch. Well, just as they went to kiss the watch, you jerked the watch up, you know, all the way. And then they'd make a Dutch wagon wheel. That was quite a thing. And then two would take a handkerchief and hold each end this way and then two more across it this way. And they'd get everybody in the, in the crowd up there with a handkerchief across that there. Usually they had men with handkerchiefs, big handkerchiefs, and they'd take them. Then the top one, the top handkerchief, they had to go under there and cross, change places. And they were supposed to kiss as they passed under there. And then they had to come back over there. Well, if they went over on this side of the handkerchief this way, if they come back on that side, they was all right. But if they happened to come back, you know, on this side to twist the handkerchief, then they had to go back again. Well, they did come back a while. There was time to twist the handkerchief, they were supposed to kiss as they went under there. And then they had to go under and all this. Then of course when they got the handkerchief straight, well them two, them two were done and the next two, next two come down and tried their luck that way. Then they'd look at the stars sometimes, you know, that was to redeem something that you hadn't.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh, yeah, they had things to redeem something to look at the stars. And that was to hold it up and you'd look through that and somebody'd pour water down it, you know, into your face.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Sometimes you'd have to bring somebody a bucket of water, a drink of water to pay for a forfeit. One time I was a party and I had to bring a young girl, a young lady a drink of water to pay for a forfeit. I went and brought the bucket. And she drank out of it. She went out and held it up for her to drink. She said, now don't pour the water, I won't, I wouldn't have poured it on her. She was afraid I would when she didn't drink a full of water.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I didn't drink that on the bottle. It really is. I used to have quite a lot of fun. And then back to the get married, of course, like most everybody at 17, 16 you think you're pretty smart anyway. Well, I thought I was alright. But there was a lot of things that if I'd been a little older I could have did better. But then there was a lot of things that I already had did, you know, with the housework and the experience with children and the washing and cooking and a lot of things. Why I, it was just learning, you know, to manage on what we had to do with and so on. We got along pretty good.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Were you, you were probably, you probably didn't mind getting out of the, getting out of your house where you'd been brought up in that house.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, I, that there was, you know, quite a bit of contention as I began to grow up. But that in his own way, in some ways, was quite good to me. But it seemed like as I grew older he had a lot of dreams or ideas that he would like for me to got on to school. And then he began to get the idea, I think the part of it would be that he realized sooner or later I was going to be gone and he thought I'd miss me at home. And he, you know, had to find pretty much fault with any boyfriends that I had. And of course that didn't help matters any. And the first one that I had I went with, why they didn't care much about him and so, but he treated me fine by the time I went with him. And my stepdad was going to just, going to stop to that right now and mom said just leave her alone, I think she'll find it out for herself. Said well we'll get further by just letting her alone. Well it wasn't too long, you know, until I didn't want anything more to do with him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of Ed at first? Huh? What did you think of Ed at first?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well Ed used to stay with him and batch with him and he found a lot of... Well after Ed and I began to pay a lot of attention to each other and...

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well I had a good, I had a good buggy and team and when we went someplace of course that was the way she got to go was because I had a team. Well they all sailed, they had lived across the potlatch but they had a hack and they couldn't get out of the team but I could take a buggy and go up to the arrow there. And they'd bring the hack, bring it across the railroad bridge, pull it across by hand and hook my team onto it and we'd go in where we wanted to, you know that. One night I went up there with the buggy.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: You got a fair bit out of the house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Huh?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: You got a fair bit out of the house.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well... Not very much. We didn't get any of the stove and we got some bedding out but I burnt all our clothes except for how I had all of them. I had a good saddle and burnt that up in there. I had it in there so it wouldn't mold in the winter, you know.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well we lost things in there. But we lived, we had a wonderful crop of apples and a cabbage patch and we'd had meat, our meat burned but lard. We had our lard in the cellar and lots of canned fruit in the cellar and big barrel crout. And so we lived out of that cellar because we didn't have any crop the next year so we lived for a year out of what. And the cellar was just little ways from the house and it was built, you know, filled with sawdust and had a shake roof on it. And it got fire on the roof but some of them took holes or something they got there, you know, shovels. Snow on it. Well, they knocked some of the shingles off and then the schoolhouse set up on top of the hill and they could see and there was kids about 12, 14, 15 in there. So the boys come and they run in the cellar to bring out the fruit. And it was in shelves and they'd just make a dash in there and just run their hands kind of, work their hands through the jars and just grab what they could. And then they'd run out, you know, and just drop it in the snow. Well he had a brother that took a sack and went in and he was sacking up a big sackful to bring out. But by the time he got by, while he was carrying the sack, the boys just in and out, you know, dodged around him and about had the cellar empty and they only broke two or three jars. But they wasn't taking time and over any sacking and dodging in and out and they really emptied that cellar. But the cellar didn't burn either. Sounds like it still must have been a real setback for you. I mean, really a blow. Well, the greatest blow was that people were awful kind and good and they'd give us things and, you know, give us clothes and we had some money and then some new materials and I happened to have a sewing machine and we managed. But we got the scarlet fever right after that. So, you know, when we moved, well then we was in quarantine and we had some cows and then some cows and my uncles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And so we couldn't ship cream because of this scarlet fever so we fed cream to the, we had hogs didn't we?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: We churned up a lot of butter and cajuns. We lost about fifty dollars on top of everything else with the cream. That's all we had but lived on, you know.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And I had these stone crocks and my uncles could use the butter too but I would churn, work it down and press it down on these crocks and then when I'd get a crock full, well I'd put a layer of salt about like that and you'd be surprised. We had a good cellar there, this place we moved to and we put that in there and the butter, we had butter for a long time. But there we were, we didn't have a dime of income when we went to the bile and hardly leave the place. Didn't leave the place for a long time. Well, when the kids... We got to 70 or 80 and boy. We got out from some clothes that was given to us. We had a suit that he wore and we really had it really rugged there for a year or two.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They call that the seven year itch? Is that what it's called? Or is it just called the itch?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, it's a lot. It's so hard to get rid of that they used to call it that.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: These people had, I believe they called it the septicina, the man that gave me the clothes but then boy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he know he had it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, they'd know they'd had it but then... They didn't realize I guess. They didn't realize it was in them clothes I guess and I wore that suit and tie got it and the rest of them got it for me. We really had a tie. I was up there and I couldn't get away and my nephew lived below. He come up to see me. I'll talk to him and ask him what to do. What he could do for me. I said, Dallin, for God's sake, I said send me sulfur. So he got me a big patch of sulfur and a batch of sulfur and sent it up to me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Dad do it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: He cleaned it up with that.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Then with our scarlet fever though, shortly after we burned out, the kids got sick and well, the girls wasn't so awful sick and they didn't break out much. Well, the minute that they broke out, we called up the health officer. That's one of the doctors at Orthea. We called him up and told him we had something and we didn't know what. We were staying at my uncle's and they had a family of children in school too. And they ought to come up and see about it. Well, he said the weather was kind of bad and everything. He said, well, I don't like to make a trip on the hill right now. I can't come for a few days, just keep them home and everything. Well, that made us all kind of sore. Mrs. Choate, the lady at the switchboard and all, the doctor Horsville was coming to Cavendish. There was a child that was awfully sick out there and he was coming to Cavendish just about every day, which was four or five miles away. But it was on top of the hill, you know. So she told Dr. Perry, why don't you authorize Dr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Horsville to come over and check them?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, he wouldn't do that. So then we waited and he'd tell us, well, you know. And so finally I called and told him that we were going to rent this place down in the canyon and time to get down there for spring work. And that we were going to go and, well, he'd be up. He'd try to be up there, you know, two or three days. Well, by that time the girls was pretty good. Virgil, why, he wasn't so sick, but he did break out. He just broke out thick. And he was still, the rash was showing on him quite a lot. And so when we moved down, first we had some cows and things that we'd gathered up, you know, to take household stuff he would give us and so on. And my uncle took his cows down there and he kind of went in with them renting the place. And so he had taken the cattle, his oldest boy and so it went along.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And Laura was Peggy with us or was Laura and Peggy both on the wagon?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: They were both on the wagon. The girls was on the wagon, I guess, with my uncle. And some of the stuff was gone. And I had a hack.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have a wagon too?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I had a wagon. Yeah, and we were kind of coming together, but I was driving a hack, you know, a bunch of stuff on in. And I had Virgil all bundled up and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And we had to go from where my uncle lived down a lane, what, about half a mile, wasn't it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, about a half a mile. Down to hit the road, main road, to take off, you know, down to a gaff of old cream ridge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And who did we meet, you know, before we got down to the main road?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Here come Dr. Farrelly. And of course he stopped in the road and he stopped and asked us where we was going. I told him, well, we told you we had the move, you know, and he'd come. Well, he says if you've got anything contagious, you can't do that. And so he asked questions and I unbuttoned Virgil's coat, shirts, you know, enough that he could see his chest. He says, oh my goodness, it's wind and everything. Get it bundled up, get it bundled up. You can't go anywhere. You've got scarlet fever. I can stop you. You can't go into another county, you know, with scarlet fever. And I said, well now, Dr. Farrelly, I said we've tried to get you to come up here and to authorize another doctor and you didn't do any of it. And I says, now, part of our children are already gone on down, pretty well down the grave with my uncle. And I says, now, no doubt legally you might be able to stop us, you know, where we've got a contagious disease. But, I says, I want to tell you something. I said, we've been on the mercy of the people since we burned out here long enough. And I said, and my uncle is keeping us, you know, and I said he isn't able to do that. And I says, maybe you can stop us. But I said, I want you to know one thing. Whenever you stop us, you've got the full responsibility of us. You're going to see that the county or yourself or somebody takes care of us. Because, I says, we've tried every way, you know, to do what's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, you know it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And I said, the people all on the Hill know it. And they've tried too. I said, there's been other people talking to you. Now, I said, I don't say that you can't stop us because maybe you can.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: But I said, when you do, I said, that's just fine. I said, when you do, we're at your mercy. I said, you're going to take care of us to see that we're taken care of. She was the one that done the talking when they told me to go. I just laughed at them, you know. I just laughed. Well, it was a, you could take it serious or you could laugh. Because there you was, burned out and out in the road. And you're going to stop you when you're sitting in the middle of the road. Then she took it up and went to doing the talking.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Afterward, he told somebody, he said she should have never married Ed Grossgloss, she should have been a lawyer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he do?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, after I talked to him, you know, and he said that woman should have never married Ed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, but I mean, what did he tell you? What did he have you do?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, what he told us, he said, well, well, he said, here, I'll give you a quarantine card. And he said, you go straight to the place where you're moving and you put this card up and you call Dr. Lyle, who was the health... Officer Nez Perce. Officer Nez Perce. And he says, you call Dr. Lyle and report your case to him. And then, you know, he'd take charge because we'd be in another county. So we did. We took him, we come on, put the sign up and called Dr. Lyle, you know, to come up and check us. And he asked about questions and I told him that we had this up, but he figured that we had scarlet fever. And I told him how long we'd been in, that the girls was over, Virgil, you know, was still having it. And he said, well, just stay in. And he said, but you don't go anywhere, you don't sell any cream, you don't let the kids go to school, you know. So the people at GAPA, the fellow there, Hoskins, had a store, post office was there. And we had a phone. We'd call... We had one put in, we didn't have it until we first went there. Well, we could call up and he would put things out of the store. He credited us, you know. And he'd set the mail and stuff out on the porch and we could go down and get it and bring it home. We stayed out of people's houses and so on. So let everybody know, you know, about it. Then I called up, and I told him, I said, well, I think that we are, you know, through this, that we would be able to go out, you know. And it had been so many weeks and so on. Oh, he said, you can't go out, there's any kind of scaling if there's anything. Well, Virgil's hands and feet had appealed, you know, and they had scaled and I'd watched until I'd quit and everybody else was all through with it and all right. And so I told him, I said, well, I think we're all through. And I said, we can't stay in here much longer. I said, we've, you know, got a cell cream and nothing to live on and other things that just were, we've got to get out. Oh, well, if you can't get out, try and get up there. I don't know when I can get up there and so on. So I waited two or three days and he didn't come up. So I got on the telephone and I called him up and I told him, I said, if you're not up here, I think that was on a Saturday, Friday night, Saturday morning. I said, if you're not up here to check us before Monday morning, I said, I'm telling you, Monday, we're going out. I said, the ed's got to get out and get stuff in the crop and I said, we're not staying in any longer. Oh, you can't do that. I said, well, we're just waiting until Monday and we're going. So he didn't show up Saturday. Sunday, well, I thought, well, I guess he isn't going to come and I'll just go out if I get arrested. So Sunday evening, just about dark, we went after the cows, all just walking out of the place, you know, to get away from the house. And they had an old iron and an apricot tree back of the house where we lived that I guess they used to call them men out of the field or something. And we heard somebody pounding on that. We could look down, you could see there was a man down at the house. So I thought, well, maybe that's him. So I took the kids and went on back to the house and they were bringing the cows. So he talked to them a little bit and he asked, you know, about the scaling and I told him, well, that he had scaled but there wasn't any more scales and hadn't shown up for a few days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said, what about his feet?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And with my hands, I held out his hand.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about his feet?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I just put the kid up on my lap and just started jerking his shoes off. And he says, oh, no, no, no. And he said, that's all right. You don't need to do that, you know. And so he said, well, you would go ahead and fumigate, clean up, and then he says, you can take your sign down and go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, now just how do I fumigate? And I said, what do you want me to do?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And he said, well, burn formaldehyde candles in each room, you know, spread our clothes out on chairs and things like that, you know, and just put those formaldehyde candles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, and where do I get the candles?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And he said, well, you know, you can buy them at the drugstore, buy them at the boost and so on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, how do we buy them?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I said, we've been in without money. And I said, we can't go anyplace to get them. And I said, maybe you'd better just get some and send to us. And I said, I'll do what you tell us, you know, if you send. Well, when I get him to understand that him or the county or somebody was going to send the candles or whatever we were to do, well, he didn't send near as many. I didn't have to do near as much as he had first told me to. But he did send them. And I did do the fumigating. And I did the fumigating years afterward, why we had the daughter that was born there, the gaffer that had the scarlet fever. Good heavens, they didn't have us to fumigate. We just had to wash the bedding, hang it out on the line, and hang the clothes out, and the sun in the air. And they put her in a room, kept her in the room for a couple weeks or so. And they let Johnny, long as we kept him out of the room, they let him go to school. And there was such a difference, I mean, the handle thing and everything, but we really went through it with the burnout and the dry year and the crop failure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it crop failure that year after that year? What happened to the crop?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, it was just so dry and people couldn't get the dried out. Just work up the clotting.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I got one dream, I didn't get some of them done, I was exposed to that scarlet fever. It rained a lot. Well, I worked plowing the rain a lot of times. But when you're exposed to scarlet fever, you don't know whether to get wet or not. That might have put you under the ground. You know, it was pretty serious. I lost quite a little time staying in the house out of the rain that I'd have been out plowing if I hadn't been exposed to scarlet fever. I don't think I had it. But there wasn't a farmer in the whole country that year that got his farming done. It rained late and then it turned hot. And three days after it quit raining, the ground was baked hard. Even the tractor man gave up and didn't get all the work done.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, that must have left you in terrible shape financially, all this stuff. You must have had no money at all. No, we hadn't. Now, the people didn't know us too well, you know, and there wasn't any reason in the world for that man Hoskins to carry us. He didn't know that he'd ever get a dime. He just took a chance and I guess he felt sorry for us, you know. Of course, we had a few chickens, we had eggs, and as I say, we had our canned stuff, you know, like kraut and lard and all like that. And we had the milk and the butter. It was the flour, you know, and things like that that we had to buy. But he was very good and when we got to where we could sell some cream, you know, not where we could sell the cream, then we began to pay him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then you finally got to work some on the road, didn't you?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Boy, at that time, butterfat was 11 cents a pound. But you could buy quite a bit, you know, at a cream shop. Of course, other stuff was down too. It wasn't like Friday now, but think about...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What period of time was this, that this happened? What period of time? Was it during the Depression or before? Well, this was when, let's see, Bonnie was born there in 1930, wasn't she?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: This was about 1928, 27. Let's see, Virgil was about four years old when we went down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did you go to Agatha? Why did you pick Agatha to go to? Agatha?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, we just seemed to find out there's a place there to rent. It's like Isabel had a place to rent down there.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And we had cows, you know, and that would be lower down in the country and it wouldn't be as hard of winters. And a pastor and then my uncle had some cows and we just wanted to kind of get down, you know, we had lots of snow up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you own anything in Tikion? Did you own any land?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, we owned 100 acres up there. I was aimed to go down there and farm this, supposed to be 60 or 65 acres there, and I figured to farm that and then go back on top and farm this other. And I had a couple of fellows that was aimed to help me up there, said they'd help me if I needed help. But I didn't get down down there and they didn't get down up there with so I never got a thing done.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: But our land that we owned up there wasn't too much of it cleared, you know, and kind of stumps.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you homesteaded that land or bought it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Bought it. At Tikion, yeah.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And we finally let it go for bills that we owned up there. I finally let it go for a store bill. I let it go for a store bill and then the fellow that old man had got it died and they settled his estate and put it up for bids. And my brother bid 401 dollars on this 100 acres and got it. They didn't take much of that land back in there at that time. But now, since that Dorishek Dam was in there, well, you could set lands worth a thousand dollars an acre in there.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: It can't worth it, but that's the deal.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Tell it.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: But it was all these years in between, you know, bad taxes and starvation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your brother being a preacher, where did he get the idea to become a preacher? Do you know?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh, he always had that idea from a boy that he wanted to preach sometimes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There wasn't some other ministers in the family?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, we had an uncle.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And then his mother was a real religious man, a man, a real religious woman. And it just naturally, you know, he had the desire to be a minister, like somebody, a farmer or a dentist or a doctor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he pretty rough on sin as a minister?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was a Lutheran, not one of these, what's called the English Lutheran, wasn't it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, yes, he was. Lutherans here, while they will smoke or they will drink some, but he absolutely was against all of that. There was none of that, you know. And of course he's been dead now for several years, but he was in his 80s, I think, when he passed away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he, what, he was a minister for 15 years?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, he was, oh, 20, 25 years. He didn't get started then. He was preaching when we got married. Yes, but then he was, you see, he was 40 years old when he got married. He was preaching when he was about 38, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he preach around here?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: He preached around here a while. And then he preached at Pullman and at Moscow and then back in North Kater, Kansas.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he in Missouri for a little bit?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And then he went to California.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he ever give you trouble then about the way you lived your life? Did he try to tell you?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh, yes, he always tried to get us to live the right way. Well, there's four in the family that did try to be religious and there's four of us that didn't.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Another brother that tried preaching some, but he never went to college and he never held a regular charge, you might say. He just kind of filled in. Then he's got a nephew that's a Methodist minister.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But his brother did go to college, the one that was a preacher?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, one that was a preacher. And then this nephew that is the minister, well then he is the son of the brother that just preached a little, didn't have a regular charge.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, he's got two.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Carol's got two. Carol is a minister. And then this Will, didn't have the regular charge, his brother. And Carol, of course, was up here, was his son. And Carol has three sons and two of those sons are ministers.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Then my oldest brother and grandson is a preacher too, Dallas. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: So there's quite a few of them.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: The fellow takes his hard luck for fun. We've had a lot of fun in our time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you figure that's part of living? A lot of hard luck? You figure that hard luck is a big part of living?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, it's like a preacher said one time, it's a good thing we don't know what we've got ahead. If we was, nearly all of us would commit suicide.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I was going to ask you one thing about, a little bit of what you're thinking about religion is. I mean, what, you know. Oh, religion, not there. I think a whole lot of religion. I used to be a pretty wild young buck. You know, talking about religion and our country here, when I was a young fellow, I didn't care anything about the Bible and folks were religious and the further I got away from it, but I found it was converted and I got to like it. Instead of staying away from church, I liked to go. Now I do like to be as let you speak. In our country here today, they got around and they talk about communism. Russia over there, oh what a terrible place it is. Well now, under the Tsar, they had the most beautiful churches in the world in Russia, but 83 % of the people couldn't read and write and all the property in Russia belonged to the Tsar and his family. And when they went to war in 1918, he had bought his cannons and his ammunition and all that stuff from Germany. And he went to war, why, the ammunition he had for the cannons wouldn't fit him and he was fighting Germany. So the Russians, well then this communist business overthrown them. And now then, they, well, they're the best armed nation in the world. We claim we don't want to say it, but we just as well admit to the truth. They are better armed and they've got more resources. You see the Tsar never used any German, any Russian resources.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I was down at the mill.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: They've got minerals and they've got everything over there. And they've got their people educated now. So we have got out here in our great nation here, they have got the Bible not allowed in the schools because it teaches religion. Well the Bible does not teach religion. You can read that Bible and you don't know nothing about it. You read through it and you won't find anything about a Lutheran or a Methodist or a Catholic or Adventist or anything in there. Till one of them fellows comes along and picks out certain passages of scripture and shows you what they do. They're their place. Now the Dunkards for instance, they at the Lord's Supper, he told them as often you do this, do this in remembrance of me. Well every religious outfit has a little different way of doing that. But the Dunkards, they go and they have a full supper. And then after supper they have a basin of water there and one of them gets up and takes a towel and ties around him. And washes one of his feet and one of his brother's feet next to him, takes the towel off and stands up and kisses his brother. Ties the towel on him and he sits down and washes one of his feet and washes one of his brother's feet. Takes the towel off and kisses him and ties it on to him and they go around the other way. Now that's their way of having the Lord's Supper. They eat, they eat as supper. But the others, they have a little piece of wine, a little piece of bread, a little bit of wine and they drink that. Somehow the Christian church, they have this sacrament every Sunday. My youngest daughter is Christian. I've been to their church quite a lot of times. The others don't have it so often. But they teach them things and then the baptism, baptiso means to put water on you some way. Well some people sprinkle it on you. A Catholic is supposed to pour it on until it runs out. But the train men, they come and eat there at the store and I happened to stop my buggy right in their trail where they come from the train over there. So when I come back my buggy was turned over laying down on the side. I didn't see why anybody had done that. I thought maybe the old crest there, some of them would have done it that way and I asked him about it. No, he said the train man done it. He said you left it in their trail that they come down, the way they come down to the store here and they just turned it over when they come to it. Come on. So that was the reason why it was turned over. But we went up in the, went from there on up to literary and the hostels hacked and backed that for her and then took the buggy.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: But that's what made the old man mad because I had the conveyance you know. She couldn't have went a lot of times. We hadn't been for the conveyance that I furnished. He didn't especially want her to go. And he didn't want her to go. And then one big mistake I made there was about him. They had a dance up at a place up the canyon there. There was nobody living at the place at that time. A bachelor. Well I had it rented and it was a pretty good house up there. So Gilbert Jamieson, he come home from school and told me about it. One of them goes, oh I haven't got no girl and I don't know nothing about it. I didn't know nothing about it in time to ask Ginger by Dixie to go with me. So I said I don't think I'll go. And one of the heroes I said I'll have some fun going up there watching the girls pull their rubbers off and going up in that mud. But that night it wasn't muddy up there. But Gilbert and I started out together. He was going to get the hostel girls to pick her up at Arrow. And as we went by Jim Austell, he'd come over there and got Dela Jamieson in the canyon there and they was going up. I hadn't aimed a stop for Wilson because I had no date. She said nothing. Well they said you better go up and ask Dixie to go. Well I guess it won't do no harm to ask her. Well when I got up there, why, there Ervin, old Larry Ervin and Harriet was there at Wilson's. That was a girl. Well there was a girl that Gilbert was going there to meet. She was there at Wilson's and Dixie was all ready to go. So I hadn't asked her. She'd have went if I hadn't went. But the old man didn't know that I didn't have a date. So he told my brother about, preacher brother about me taking her to an old outhouse for a dance. When I didn't have any ideas she was going and I didn't have anything to do with the girls until me and Harriet went together and we started up the hill. And then the girls, they got to tease me about what the school mom had said about me. If I just knew what she'd said. And then they wouldn't tell me. I don't think she said anything. They'd just make enough of something.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh she might have. Holly used to say quite a bit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Huh?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Holly used to say quite a bit. Well I don't know whether you'd heard anything or whether you hadn't or anything. But anyway, you guys wouldn't tell me. And there was grades, steep grades along there and I'd tell them you don't tell me what you say. I'd shove them over the grade and I'd grab the two of them and take them over the edge of the road. You know from there on up, going on up with the girls, I didn't know she was away at home at 10 o 'clock. No it was 12. I was to be home by 12. By 12, well, whatever time it was I didn't know anything about it until Harry come around and got Harriet and he said, you know, Dixie's supposed to be home at a certain time.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I asked Harriet to go because them days and everything, I was pretty particular about what people thought about you, your reputation. I knew if I was going to get to go again and Mom told me to be home by 12, I'd better be home by 12. Well I was married a couple of fairs, you know, to doing. So a lot of them, you know, was having a good time yet and I had to leave by 11 .30 to be home by 12. So nobody said anything about breaking up or going and I knew I had to go and I wasn't much going to go by myself, you know, or anything. So I went and asked Harriet, I said I don't like to do this but I said I don't want to go along. She said well ask Ed. I said well folks would have a fit, you know, if they know the dance or the party was still going and Ed and I took off by ourselves. Well the first thing I knew about Harriet said it. If she would mind coming and they were kind enough and good enough that her and her uncle come and come with Ed and I. So when I got home, my dad didn't happen to hear me come in. I went in very quietly. So he happened to wake up and he said something to Mom about the dixie aunt and got home and Mom said well she's here. She's been here in bed quite a while and he let the laugh and brought, come to see if I was there and I was. But I always was not bragging on myself or anything like that but then I always tried to do, get home and you know do what, basically what Mom told me to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he give you a hard time about that later?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, no he didn't say anything. He proved himself. He proved to himself, of course I'd come home and gone to bed and she told me I could go and everything. Well it just wasn't every time that he'd have a spell about it but just now a man that he would get real disturbed about my going. And I think the older I got, as I grew older and looked back on it, I think that the idea was that he hated the idea when I did leave. And then when he found out I was going to get married, well I had kind of threatened to go somewhere and try to work. I didn't really want to stay at home. And so he was going to see that I didn't get married until I was 18 but he had never adopted me. And so therefore, you know, he didn't really have any legal rights as to what I did or didn't do. And so he couldn't do much about it.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well I've seen that. And it wasn't that he disliked Ed because Ed was a master and Ed was fine until we got married. A lot of young fellas in my time, you know, that are young, but they were rounder, kind of rounders in their days. And when our families growed up, especially their daughters, they were always, always, always suspicious of them and awful particular. And that's the way it went. I don't know if I like he was telling about getting the old maids drunk and getting out and having quite a time when he was young and all that. Well, his stepdaughter growed up, well he just figured she just as wild as the women he used to chase. He didn't want that. He was special.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you look up to Ed a lot when you were, he was so much older than you, six, seven years?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Seven years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find yourself looking up to him like he was a big brother or something like that?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: He seemed like he loved you. Well I don't really know and think much about it.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I just began to like him real well and I don't... He had taxes. I married, yeah. But I didn't have any trouble as far as that having boyfriends. I had two or three of his cousins that tried to go with me. And, you know, I don't know, now Kimberlyn. And there was guys quite a bit older, even the Ned.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Ed was older than you, wasn't he?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Fred Walker, is that your name?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: But there's several even older ones that ask me, you know, to go with them. Well I knew they were enough older that even Mom and Ed, I knew the guys and all liked them all right. But I'd say, oh gee, I can't, you know, date you because the folks would just never stand for it because there's just too much difference in the age that way. But for no older than I was, marrying them, I started out with the boys when I was thirteen and there in the, I think about four years, why, I had lots of chances.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that true for your girlfriends as well? Were they going out about the same age as you started? Were they going with older boys?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Most of my real close girlfriends was, what, Harriet wasn't too much older, or a year maybe.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Gail was the one that was a couple of years older than you.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And the others wasn't too much, you know, Bert and May and them was too much difference in the ages. And the rest of them, his person that he's talking about taking for a buggy ride, well, her and I were both born 1900, she was born the 2nd of February, me the 25th. And she was a fairly good looking girl and quite a sporty girl and she just had all kinds of boyfriends.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: So... There's one time we started up the literary with the buggy and I knew that I was to take a couple of the hostels along. So I took a heck of a gig with Jamison, a saddle horse to ride up to the literary so you wouldn't have so many on the fore in the buggy. And we got up there, why there was a girl there, she must have weighed 180, there from Montana. She used to leave here and she come up there. So we got up there, why there she was. There's three girls, Jim Hostel and myself and Gilbert, there you see, there's that many of us in the top buggy and a saddle horse. So being this big Montana girl we got in the seat and Della sat on her lap and Dixie sat on mine and Harriet sat on their two knees in the middle out in the front and drove the team. And Jim and Gilbert both rode the saddle horse. That's the way we... We had that buggy fell that time. And we got up there, there's one place on the road was cold and the road just froze over with ice. Slick too but I had a sharp -scored team. And there was some brush down below the road and as we went over that ice there, got down there, them brush raked the side of that rig and I knowed that was too far down there. And I told the girl, I said pull the upper line. I didn't feel like I was scared but boy I could just feel my head raking on the rocks down below there. Because I knowed when them brush was raking that buggy that... Well there must have been ice up there that held it up level because they was way down below the road. And Jim and I raked the side of that buggy.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: They had lots of, quite a lot of, literaries then and we, one time we walked, what was it, about five, five miles or so, we walked up to literary and back.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah I wasn't alone that night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well that's how far it was wasn't it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was it that you had literary school?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Where this concrete school house is up here. Right down here. Down here above Johnson's. Johnson's Place. Pilot Rock. Pilot Rock. That's where it was.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: That's where we had most of the literaries.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Pilot Rock.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: They had debates and had a lot of fun. One time I was on the debate team and I don't know who else it was and it was whether concrete or wood would work the best for rarely. And they would have to have a narrow tie, you know, which would be the better. And sometimes it would be sensible things and sometimes it was just silly things that would come up. And he had an older brother, Levi, and he was real good at debating and the thing and he got in on something and a lot of times it is and real good.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well they had Dan Huyvark and Marion and Levi and Grover was awful good. I never was much good but I always liked them. Sometimes they would have kind of little programs, little skits or songs. Sometimes they would have somebody say speeches or sometimes sing or something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they have party games too?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well I don't think they had as much party games as they were. Not much party games. Sometimes they would have a mock trial. A mock trial.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: One time they had one up there. They had me arrested for beating up on a fellow for stealing my sister -in -law's affection. They brought that fellow in there, the hand, the arm bandaged up and the eye black and he was a badly crippled up fellow. I beat him up for stealing my sister -in -law's affection.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I had to have a lawyer. Then they had to be sworn in. Fred Albright, he was a clerk, they had to swear him in. They had to hold up your right hand and tell him they swear that everything that you told was a lie and nothing but a lie would ease your conscience. That's why you took the oath for that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But ease your conscience?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then they put on a trial?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah. They'd have to tell you no. Well they accused me of beating this fellow up and they had him up there and I didn't know it wasn't me at all. I hadn't been in touch with him. He was an old man. He was an old man, Kimberlin, that would beat him up towards me. Yeah. They had quite a time. I haven't witnessed, you know, one say that he was there and another say he wasn't. I don't know where I was that night. I wasn't around there. I wasn't supposed to be there. I was off somewhere else. I was a witness, you know, telling where I was. I wasn't around that place tall that night. I was somewhere else, rigging a horse. It was a safe story, you know.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: They had one at Arrow one time. Yeah, they had one at Arrow.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: They had my brother arrested for stealing a fella's hogs.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: The guy really had hogs and everything, you know. They made a pretty good trial out of that though. It wasn't so much silliness. But it was a lot of fun, a lot of things that went on, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Levi had studied law, eh?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: He was pretty good with the words. Oh yeah.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And good at writing and he wrote a beautiful hand. He was Bell Grosskopf's father. And Gene Grosskopf's dad.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: But after the flu scare in 1800, there never has been a literary organization since.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: When that big flu epidemic went through and killed so many people. They stopped all gathering in the church and everything and all. Nobody could stay, could have any gathering of any kind for quite a while there to stump out that flu.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How bad did that hit around where you lived?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, it hit pretty bad. I had it when my second child... Well, too many that really died, but we know some that come near by... Well, when I got it, they found out what to do. You had to take aspirin to keep your fever down and take something to keep your bowels well open. And you had to stay absolutely in bed. You had to keep yourself clean and cleaned out and keep your temperature down. And they got to doing that while they didn't lose many patients after that. But they didn't know doctors or nobody knows what to do on the start and they lost a lot of people. We had a woman got sick across the river down there and the river was high. And she had the flu and the doctors were busy and couldn't get one to come.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And she was awful sick. So her husband come over and she says she can't take oil or she can't take salt. She said, I don't know what to give her. Well, I said, get her some syrup of figs. So I went up to Kendrick's, the drugstore. I believe it was Kendrick I went to. Kendrick or Juliet anyway. I got her a bottle of syrup of figs and some aspirin. Brought it back and he come across and took it over there. She come out of it alright.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did you figure that? Why did you figure syrup of figs?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, that was kind of a laxative. That was a laxative, you know. And for something good tasting, the kids would like to take that. It was a laxative. It was an awful good laxative. I used to keep it on hands all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many people there ever get the flu in 1918?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, they had it and I don't know. Quite a few had it. I don't believe there was any deaths exactly from the flu. But they had an awful time to get along. The families would all get down. One couldn't hardly get up, put a stick of wood in the stove or wait on another.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And somebody else would just have to go and carry the wood in and kind of help out because it would really knock you through a loop. They talk about flu now, but I had at that time down there and I kept a sack of Epsom salts sitting with a bed. And I'd have such a terrible taste in my mouth. I'd reach over and that syrup of salt would get in my finger like that. Hold them in my mouth. Now them things tasted good by the side of the...that nasty taste was in my mouth. I don't know how many times I filled my mouth up with salt before I got so I could get around.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And a lot of times, you know, they'd have the nose bleed so bad. You can hardly stop it, you know, they died from that. And we had one friend up at Tekin that he had it so bad and he had the doctor up there several times. And his nose would bleed until they couldn't hardly stop it. So the doctor was there one day and told him, his name was Ed too, said, Ed won't be here in the morning, told his wife, you know the thing. You can expect him to go sometime tonight because he was so low and they was having trouble, you know, with his nosebleeds.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Somebody during the meantime told him to take a powdered alum and sniff it up your nose. Well, when they found out, they found... They found out about that and they give him some of that and he snuffed that up his nose and that stopped the bleeding and he come out of it and got all eyes.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: But his neighbor or somebody had heard tell of it, you know, and they tried it and it did stop the bleeding.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, that was one other thing that come along with the bleeding of the nose.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: He was the doctor and of course he figured they called him, I guess something about telling Ed was gone. But they called him and told him that he was still there and he said, I can't believe it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But he come out of it and lived to be, what, 81 years old, wasn't it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, he had been dead much over a year now, I guess. About your age. He was about a year older than me.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, anyway, you know, after they got that bleeding stopped and he was so weak, you know, that the doctor just hardly believed it was possible that he lived.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, we have what they call a set of flu every year, so I'm thinking about every year. You get sick, you got the flu, but it's not like that was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Besides the taste in your mouth, what was the symptoms that you had? Besides that awful taste in your mouth, what were the symptoms?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, you'd ache with that flu. You'd ache. You'd ache and hurt all over, you know, and then you have your fever and sometimes chills and then you go into pneumonia and have all these.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, it's hard to tell what you would do if you hadn't got right in your nose right on the start and you got that, holding that fever down and with aspirin.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this in 1918 that you got it or was that later?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Eighteen. You got it the same time. You got it later. Yeah, it was in the 18 when the epidemic broke out, you know, it was real bad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you live at Tekin for very long?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: About ten years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you finally sell out or what did you do? Why did you leave?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, I... We burned out. Burned out. The reason I left, went down on the river down at Dagatha and then I didn't raise no crop that year. Lost a crop up there I didn't get harvested the year before. It was a bad year. Nobody had much crop. And then burnt it out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did that happen? Did the house burn down?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, it was a rented house. We had a cabin live back in the woods and we rented this house and moved out to the settlement. Had it a quarter of a mile of school. And then, you know, the house didn't have a flue in it. It had just old pipes that went up through and it was a log house. And then there was a kitchen built across the side of it that was built on with lumber. It was kind of like a lean -to, you know, like a shed. And the stovepipe went up through that and there was a little attic up over it. And the people that had owned the house, they had lots of mail orders them days, you know, and everything parcel post. And they had this Excelsior that was packed around things and then they had little, like these 18, 20 pound fruit boxes. And up in this little attic over the kitchen, they had that place just stuffed full of those things. And Ed said one time, he says, I want to clean all that out. And I said, well, you better not do it without asking, we just rented a year at a time, you know. I said, you better not without asking Mrs. Garrison about it. Well, we had lived there a year. And when we burned out. See, you'd had one that didn't crop and you had another one in. We had two winters and it went out in March the next spring. And the only thing that we can figure out, well, we had had breakfast and I had stacked up the dishes. And then I got the girls ready to go to school and comb their hair and get them ready. And we had had the three children then and Virgil was about three years, three years old.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, they had the, had had the epic, attic on the top of the house. They'd had it full of stuff up there. But I went and took a lot of that stuff out, out of there, but still there was a lot of boxed stuff up there. This one first story of the house was logs and the next one was boards, second story. And they set a tube of four on top of these logs and then boarded up on each side with lumber. And then built this lean -to kitchen, come in this way, you know, and fastened on to them tube of fours. Well, they had, I heard said that if you take a horse manure and put formaldehyde on it and roll it in a groundhog hole and stop the whole shot, it would kill ground, ground dogs. So I had some formaldehyde and an old gunny sack and lots of rat holes in the cellar. So I cut some pieces of gunny sack and put formaldehyde on them and shoved them in them rat holes and stomped them shots.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In the cellar?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: In the cellar. But I never had got up over the kitchen to take any of that old stuff out of there. And just a few days after that, why, we hear the roar.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, there's a guy that we took in, a crippled guy that the county was going to pay us to take care of him. And he had, we moved his things, they still stored on the front porch. And he and Ed were busy talking about something that had happened in the neighborhood. And I went in and got the girls ready and got them off to school. And I'd stacked my dishes before I did that. And then I thought, well, Jess has talked to somebody and heard quite a bit about it. I'll sit down and listen, you know, because I didn't hear about it, the two of them talking and they talked to somebody. So I was listening, you know, to that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And all at once I thought, well, what is that?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Kind of a roar. And the stove in the dining room where we were wasn't, you know, any unusual fire. So I went in the kitchen and raised up the lid on the cook stove and the fire was right there out. You know, there was no big fire. The stove was pretty near cold.
