speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I thought, well, what?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And then I could hear this roar in the bottom of my head. I couldn't see it, the ceiling, you know. But all at once I realized, you know, that, so I screamed, you know, the house is a fire. So, of course, Jess was kind of crippled up. He just grabbed his coat and hat and got out of the way. And he put the birds on, held on to him to keep him back. And, of course, Ed told me he, well, he went out and jumped up on the shed roof, told me to hand him the ax. And I handed him the ax.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, there's a well there, but it was so hard. I figured I'd chop a hole through the roof, you know, and get water up there. But when I got up there, I could hear this fire going up, up this way. Well, that, it gets like a stovepipe, you know, up that way. And when I heard that, I knew there was no chance. I said, throw out the junk. It's gone.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, you told me to go up the telephone. Oh, the telephone, the neighbors. But there was a five gallon can. We always kept something to get some snow water. And that can of water, five gallon was what I could get to. And he told me to hand him the water. Well, I picked up a five gallon can of water up in one hand and held it up. Picked up on the roof. Nothing to it, you know. He got it, Matt didn't do much, and he got the ax. Well, I never, I never chopped through because I heard that fire going up the wall.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I knew that there was absolutely no chance to get ahead of it.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And so I ran to the telephone. What they did, the party lines, everybody rubbernecked, you know, and everything. And if there was any unusual ring, well, people know there's something wrong. Just one of the wrong rings. I just went and gave a lot of just crazy rings, short rings, a long ring, and a lot of jingles. And when I began to hear the receivers come down, why, you know, I don't know who it was, and the house was on fire. And of course a lot of people could see, lived around and sight, and could see a lot of people got there pretty soon. And we got quite a few things out of the house.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Up there, the old church is still there, but I don't know any of them anymore. We went to the Friends of the Church. So they, you see them, them religions, them religions are taught what I believe, what you believe, and what somebody else believes. And they read that scripture and read it straight through. Like Philip, when the eunuch was reading the scripture, the Apostle Philip heard him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He went out and he said, do you understand what you're reading? He said, how can I unless some man explains it to me?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: So Philip got in the rig with him and explained the scripture to him, and then they found some water. Well, he said, here's some water.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What hinders me to be baptized?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, he went down to the water and was baptized. Well, the people that claim that you're supposed to be immersed, people that have been there say there's not enough water at that place to immerse a cat. Maybe there was more water at the time. Well, I don't know what was there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you had the experience yourself of being born again? Huh?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, I know what that is. That is where I've been a -talkin' about our country here, trying to tell how much, what a good country they are. And then taking the Bible, I claim, and I know there's a lot of people like me that don't care for it. And the scripture says that it was to teach it, write it on your porch and write it up and teach it to your family all the time. Well, then I claim that our Bible should be put in the school as a reader in the fifth grade. Yeah, you were telling me about that. That's what I figure, that it should be in the fifth grade as a reader. Now, then you get our congressmen and people like that to do that. It was mostly history. I mean, it's kind of a history. Well, there's an awful lot of history in it. Of course, you believe in God and what he meant by that. I heard an infidel say that he read it three times. He'd never seen anything what you could do very well without. But then here's the proposition about seeing what you can do without. We have laws in our country here that wrote against murder and all this kind of things there. But the first law God wrote with his finger to Moses, not to murder and not to lie and not to steal and not to commit adultery and all them things there. God wrote them first. Now, that's like our automobiles. Henry Ford started out tinkering with stuff and he got a thing that would run on its own power. Well, he didn't know. There'd never been a Ford and never been a car first. But Ford got in there and figured that out and made that motor and got a thing to run. Well, now there's all kinds of motors, cars, and things like that. Well, we turned in and experimented and we made the atomic bomb. And after World War I, they had a conference and they agreed unanimously that there would be no military secrets between nations. In England, in the United States, one or two of them said to the other, We can't share the secret of the atomic bomb with Ruchi. The other said, We can't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now then, who built the Iron Curtain?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Ruchi's got the atomic bomb. We had it first. They knowed that there was an atomic bomb and they got scientists and got together and they built it. Well, China's built it. India's got it. And they've got that atomic bomb all over now. But the first one that was built was very hard to fix. Same way as an infidel came in and said, We can do without anything that's in the Bible. But the law that we all have to go by, or supposed to go by, of not murdering and not stealing and lying and the Ten Commandments, God wrote them first. Well, every nation in the world has got them laws in the law books. They're fine. You don't have to go to the Bible to find them anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But the first one, who wrote the first one? And who built the first this and who built the first that?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I know the first phonograph I ever seen. I was a little kid in Virginia and there was a fellow come along, traveling through the country and stopping at school houses and he had a few, a bunch of records on an Edison phonograph and he had a horn that was supported by a stand that stood out in front of it. It was a heavy horn. It had to be supported on that. I remember that. And I was small enough that my dad, we only had a mile to go, and my dad packed me part of the way home. So you know I was a pretty small lad when I seen the first phonograph. But there's lots of different, one different thing now made since Edison started that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the electric light, how long did he work to do that?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: But Edison made the first one. Now then, most anybody, lots of people can make them things. Well the thing that I wanted to ask you about, about this religion, is that I was curious about how it was important to you when you were an adult or a long time ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean was, did you find that the idea of, was the idea of being born again something that was important to you? In those years? The revivals and all that? Is that important to you personally?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, as we grew up we liked to go to the revivals and most of us have been to the altar and all like that. But as a child I was always in church, you know, and now they were Sunday. Always in church. We were raised, you know, with the background or to go to church and so on up until you got up in your teens, you know, to feel responsible for your own religious welfare. And of course we make a lot of mistakes and don't live up to it like we should. But we believe it and we have been to the altar, but I don't know, we've had a feeling of some of them call it the Holy Spirit or sanctification and some of that by...

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Some people have them ideas of calling it one thing and then another. The Lutherans call their, what some people call sanctification, they call it growing in grace. Getting... Trying to get better. The longer you live, the longer you're trying to, not to get any worse at least, get better all the time and live closer to God and every way that's the way. Well, some of them they talk about their sanctification and well after they get it I can't see a better difference in them. After they get what they call sanctification, they can but then they did before. They may feel different themselves, you know. Yeah, the inward feeling I don't know. I can only tell my inward feeling, I can't tell yours. And...

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Then I know some people, I do things that they wouldn't do. Now I have a nephew up here that... They do things that I wouldn't do and I've seen some of them that I know business like and everything that I've seen them go up for sanctification and you feel real good about it, you know. It's going to be wonderful. But in their daily lives and in their business dealings, why it doesn't coincide with my idea of what. Do you think it...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you feel like it's the way it makes you act more than the way it makes you feel? You know what I mean?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I mean, is it...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you look at religion as something that's in the way that it makes you live your life? The way you treat other people? Is it the way it affects the way you, you know, deal with people?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, I think it has a lot of ways. It should have a lot of dealing and a lot of ways, you're dealing with people. But now you're thinking about that, it takes me back to the Adventists that lived in Arrow a long year ago. And there's a fellow come down the road with a team and got stuck in about 20 rods of this Adventist house on Friday evening after sundown, after dark. And they went to the Advent to get his team to pull him out up the little hill that wouldn't have been... There wasn't 20 rods to the top of the hill. But no, it was his Sabbath and he wouldn't take his team out. He wouldn't go and help that man. They went out to help him. But my brother was teaching school at that time and he went down to... Mrs. Jordan was his clerk of the board and he went there to get his warrant for his month's teaching that happened to be that night. And then there was a fellow growing man there that was going with the Jordan girl and Jordan and my brother and the man that had the wagon. The four of them went and took the load off of the wagon and packed it up to the top of the hill so the team could pull the wagon up and then load it again. And he went about a quarter of a mile further. The only place in the country that had a barn next to the road except the Adventists. And he put up there for the night with that fellow. So that was the way they... Well that fellow says, I would take my team out to pull half a man out after God on my Sabbath. And Jesus got took apart because he healed a man on the Sabbath day. And he asked them, why if any of them had an ox and a dick if they wouldn't take it out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They picked the corn on the Sabbath too didn't they?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah they talk about corn and I don't know what kind of stuff it was. They had corn, some kind of something. They have something they call Jerusalem corn. That's kind of a head like cane. And then they had wheat. But corn, what we know as corn, was originated in America.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well we believe it's good to... it's alright to do good on the Sabbath. But just not to sit around all week and leave everything and then try to get it all done on Sunday. But if anybody is in need, you know, I wouldn't hesitate to stop.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well the old fellow used to live over here. There was an Adventist. He used to live up in the same country. And he used to talk about going out to churches in Leland. They used to have two Methodist churches there. But he'd talk about going out to church there. Well now you come out just before 12 you know. And he'd say, it seemed like that Adventist would have it worked out every day to pass the church just as they come out. They'd have a load of wood. They'd go on with a load of wood. He hadn't worked on Saturday but then on Sunday he'd be passing this church. I think the Adventists and all of them are very sincere, very good people.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And they do a lot of good.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: In a way they do a lot of good. On the Sabbath, you know, to help you. But I've heard a lot of people say, I don't understand the Bible. Now there's this one thing here, a preacher, an old Methodist preacher had been in Alaska, a missionary. He said the Catholics come up there in Alaska and the Methodist and then the Adventists come up there. Well he said them people didn't know what to do. The Catholics was a different idea, the Methodist and then the Methodist and different to the Adventists.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think all these denominations get in each other's way? They get in each other's way, all these different denominations?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: They do. They do get in each other's way more with men that are not religious because a man that's not religious, he sees this one and that one and the other and they all tell you what to do. Now I got a cousin, or did have a cousin, that was a Seventh -day Adventist. His father was a strong Lutheran and a good man. And this fellow, he left Virginia between two nights, between two days.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And he come into Mexico and there he got turned to be an Adventist. And they had him arrested for the stubborn worship. And I've got his picture here, it was sent to his mother, to my mother, that was his aunt, you know. His picture, looking through the iron bars, he's in jail and it said, Blessed are you when you are persecuted for my name's sake. Well my oldest brother went east with Uncle Jim Castle, that is this fellow's dad, come out here. And then he went back around visiting his boy and my brother went with him. And he went east and my brother went back and got married. So my dad told him after this come out, he said, Now Levi, I want to ask you something about Kelly Castle's place there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had he got a blacksmith shop and isn't there a church on the other corner, on the other side of the street, right from his blacksmith shop?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: He said, I believe there is. I said, that's it. I know Jim Castle, he was a fellow I found, I used to go by his place and he'd spit on the handle and put a hot iron on it and hit it to make a racket to scare my horse. Bailey Castle is working in his blacksmith shop right across the street from that church. While they were having worship on Sunday, he was disturbing that worship by working in his shop. And there he had it up there, his picture looking through the bars, Blessed are you when you are persecuted for my name's sake.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was he thrown in jail? Whereabouts was he thrown in jail?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: This was somewhere in New Mexico.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Then that would be, he would be in Adventist, he'd do his work on Sunday.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, let's see, what was I going to ask you?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I was going to ask you something about Adams, this guy in Juliet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear about his deal with that wheat?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Oh, Adams wheat, yes sir, I don't hear that wheat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you hear about that wheat? What did you hear about it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, he said that he got that found it in a wild goose's crow in Alaska. But there was a fellow, Lee Hall, lived down in the, had a homestead up the little potlatch canyon and he was a great hunter. And he said he went up on the Genesee Ridge and it looked like the wheat had flowed out or something and he went up there and it looked like it had been drilled crossways and this here peculiar wheat was in that field. And the heads of that wheat was like this, kind of double and bearded. One head seemed to be a little shorter than the other, but it was a big head, I don't know if it had sold. Well, it's like some wheat, you know, some of them the heads are awful tight and shorter and others are looser, look like bigger heads and yet they don't have any more grain in them than the other. Well, that was kind of the way it is, it was a looser head of wheat than the other and had a big head there. But this hall he brought that home and planted it in his garden, well that will grow, you know, one grain of wheat will make a great big bunch. That fall he brought a handful of it up and put some in the bank, that peculiar wheat. He had some on him in his hand and he got kind of mad at old Adams and he thought he'd just tell him a good yarn, you know. He met him on the street and said, Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Adams would you like to have a few heads of the flandike wheat?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: That was a great gold country up in Alaska country then. A few heads of the kondike wheat, he says you plant in your garden and it will produce 160 times what you plant. Well, any wheat will do that. You plant it in there and it grows good, why you wouldn't want to make a whole handful of heads there. I believe it would beat that most any wheat is doing on good ground, get one grain put in place and keep the weeds out from around it. But Adams, he took it and he done that and he started it, kept multiplying from that little bunch that Hall gave him until he got enough to get acreages of it. And then he put up this story of Alaska wheat found in a goose's crawl and got to selling it for $20 a bursal for seeds. Well, he shipped some to Kansas back there from here. And then the government got to thinking about such an off -landage price as that, you know, for wheat. Ordinary wheat then wasn't, oh I don't know, whether they ever got 80 cents a bursal. But consider the general run of wheat at 80 cents a bursal and then this stuff here at $20 a bursal, the government went to investigating to see what it was. And when they actually found out where it comes from, it was the old Egyptian wheat, the oldest wheat in the world.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This is what Hall had gotten up off the ridge, was this old Egyptian wheat?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: That was what it was. He was the one that got the original kernels of stuff, but this Adams said he got down the goose's crawl.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know Hall?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he tell you that's where Adams got it?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was he mad at Adams? Was there any special reason?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, he was working on the mill, putting a roof on it, some kind of a combination roof and it had some wire in it, like. He gave Adams a piece of it and he wanted to know how you cut it. This made Hall mad. This made Adams mad because Hall gave him this stuff and he dulled his knife trying to cut it.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: That kind of made Hall too, so he just thought he was a pretty good guy and wanted to make up pretty good yarns, Hall was. So he told him this and then he changed it from Klondike to Alaska and then he made himself a lot of money out of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Adams really believe that this was the Hall story that was so he asked for wheat?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: No, he changed the story around, I don't believe Hall told him. Maybe Hall did tell him it had come out of the goose's crawl. But Hall didn't tell him it was Alaska, he told him it was Klondike.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't Adams say he found it up in Alaska himself?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I believe he did, he claimed he got it himself out of the goose's crawl.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they stop him from selling or what happened?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, after they found out it was the old Egyptian wheat, then they stopped him from selling it and stopped him from shipping it out of the country. Well, he had a son -in -law that was a banker up here. He was a poor guy. Yeah, and the two of them, they shipped a carload of hay from here to Kansas. And they thought that was funny, shipping hay from here to Kansas. So they inspected the car and found it lying with this wheat inside of the hay. And that was smuggling. So I don't know what to find for smuggling is. But I never heard of them shipping anymore.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Did E .W.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Porter get in trouble for that, do you think?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I don't know. I don't know what happened. That's all I heard about it. They called them smuggling that wheat out of here in that hay. And I don't know what find they got or anything about it. I don't know anymore about that. Did you run across any, during the First World War, I know there's a lot of anti -German feeling. I mean, people around here kind of disliked the German people for a while because of the war. I know like they wasn't supposed to speak the German language and that kind of thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear about that during that time?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: There was none of them around here. Well, you know, one thing I was going to ask you about coming from Virginia was that I know there's some of this bitterness about the Civil War and all, the grand old army and all that stuff.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And I was wondering if anybody in your family's ever had the idea that some of the people around here still held some kind of grudge against Southerners or didn't like Southerners because they were from the South.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, I don't know what they'd do anymore or not. I mean back in the early days, 1910. Back in the early days, yes they did. Some of them had something to say about the Southerners. One time there was a young fellow who wanted to run for road boss and he come up to Albright's down here and put up a hand in the north to note for them they wasn't at home. And told them to come down and vote for him and help beat the Southerners. And my cousin, Ben Crabtree, he went up there to get them to vote for him and found the note and put his name on it too when he come back and told them that this fellow went up there to try to get to be road boss to get everybody to come to help beat the Southerners. So that made everybody that was from the South mad. Two or three of them was going to run for road boss and they all drawed off for Crabtree except one, Wilson didn't. But they beat this other fellow way outside. He had no show at all as being road boss. He might have got it if he hadn't put that Southerner talk in there because the others might all have went ahead and run.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well there was lots of people that did.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Differences didn't like Southern people, Southern people didn't like Northerners. It was a long time to get it ironed out. Like Leland having the two Methodist churches in it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what Leland is now don't you?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: It had a steam run flour mill and we had a school and a post office. Well that's dark. Two good general stores and the hardware. It had two churches because one was Northern Methodist and one was Southern Methodist. One sat on this block here looking at the street this way and the other one sat on this block here looking this way there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the people claimed, well it's claimed they went by one night and they were singing in one, Will there be any star in my crown?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And the other one was singing, No, not one.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well it wasn't only the Methodist, it was nearly all the people. All the churches were split for a while over that. And somebody's bright idea of picking up that old scripture that Noah was writing which had left. So I went out in the corner and sat down. I wanted to do what was right. You know if a fella got just so drunk he wouldn't care. He wouldn't care whether he went right or left or that was alright he'd just be having a lot of fun anyway. Yeah this was a quick grill dance. Yeah if you had all the cadre of the alamander left and you turned to the right, that would tangle up the whole business. But if you were drunk enough to really drunk, well you wouldn't care.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: That's what made him bad. He never wasn't used to drinking or smoking either. And they slipped out and found a bottle and was drinking a bunch of them. Some guy that he had out in the barn and then they smoked cigarettes when they wasn't used to smoking. To kill it on the breath and come in around the girls.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well the other fellas though they all felt it too that did smoke. But I found out afterwards that that's the cheapest drunk you can have is to smoke or drink. Now smoking and drinking together will set your head a whirling. Yours because you're not used to it. Well I do and will other people. I was going to ask you one thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you like to write poetry? Why do you like to write poetry?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: I don't know many people that do write poetry. Well he never considered poetry I don't think. Oh I don't know. He just got them by the hand to make up rhymes. Just got a few ideas in my head and then just got to writing them away. Just every once in a while. No I don't know. A fella that married my niece he was quite a poet. And he wrote quite a lot of pretty good poems. And he says that before you write a poem he was a well educated man. He says you've got to have an inspiration to write before you can do that. Well there's times you know. Well there's times that I think there's something that will make a rhyme and keep a rhyme in there a little ways. And then at other times why I couldn't write three words that would rhyme and save your life.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: It's just some foolish idea. I try to make a joke out of things. And then when he like worry I'm always the big worry worker. Well I guess he worried but he didn't say very much about it. He just could make himself more or less accept.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was that?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: You.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: You could just accept it and go on you know. He's quite a hand to figure that the other fella had rights the same as he did. And I know that one time we all needed work so bad. And there's several guys after the job and a little road work come up. And I insisted that he try to get on. And well he said there's a lot after. And he said you've got two brothers that's after. And he said if they can get on they've got your mother to take care of. And mama was an invalid practically so. And the brothers you know they growed up why they had to do the supporting. And so he said everybody can't get on. And he said we can't all be on there. And he said if they don't get on I said your mother's going to suffer. And so he said if there's room for all fine. But he said I'm not going to. So Val Merit Arrow one time you said you know you wasn't going to push anybody out you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this road, railroad or just road work?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: This was before he went on to the railroad. It sounds like this road work was one of the only jobs a person could get.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this WPA stuff?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: WPA or PWA or whatever they called it. They had that a while, NRA.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: And then they'd get a few days each. They'd give you ten, give one fellow ten days and then another one ten. So everybody could have a little bit more. That was after Roosevelt come in and started up giving people a little work you know, a road worker. Well then it wasn't all road work. He went to making these trails for the country too. And people got on that and made roads or trails all over the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that that was a real good thing to happen for Roosevelt to get in and start trying to change?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yes, he done a lot of good things. Made that a lot more. He opened up business. Now Hoover, they always make fun of him. But what Hoover done, he appropriated a lot of money and gave it to the wealthy men to hire. They were supposed to go and hire the poor people and put them to work. But instead of them doing that, they went and repaired their machinery, their factories. Made them better and never hired any outside work. So that left the people worse off than ever and everybody hated Hoover. But, of course. And when he got in there and made an appropriation of money, he put it out so that he knowed the poor man was going to get it. He put it out there so now there was a few places that they goofed up a little on that up here at Lenoir. There was a man up there that he was a, oh he had something to do with handling that money. But he got his son, he was a merchant. His son was supportive. He wasn't needy because his father had stuff. But he got his son on as timekeeper. And timekeeper worked straight time, you know. All through he didn't work ten days and out. The timekeeper worked all through so he got his son in as timekeeper on the job. Well I got in, I think I got in twice. I got two each at ten days while the job was going on. But this son of his, he got in there and then he was assistant foreman and he didn't know a thing about the job. I was working there one day putting some road in. I had worked on the road before and I knowed what a one to one and a one to three slope was on a fill or a cut. A one to three was a bank here, one foot slope to three feet up the bank. And the other one to one, that was the road that come out here to the road from the ditch. I knowed how to make them but he come along and started to tell me one day about it. Well he said perhaps you know more about it than I do. Well I did. And that was all he said. There was a one to three and a one to one. And he said perhaps you know more about it than I do and he went on. But then that was it. He was a boss and he didn't know what a one to one and one to three was. Well they had an old railroad man that had worked on there and he come down and started to make some of that. And he didn't understand what a one to one and one to three was. And then the boss, he didn't understand stakes, reading stakes and things like that. And he got my brother in law, George Wilson, to come up there and be a work on the job. He kept him on there quite a while because he had worked on road work and he understood all stakes and all slopes and all stuff like that. Well he could, the man that he worked for for years as a road contractor, he would send George out to finish any job work that he had. But he done most of his work in Washington. So they had, when they put this road in across the river over there, why they had some work over there to be done that George could do. You know now, a young fella, he went over there and told them and I don't know what the fella or what the boss told him, but it just so took George back that he never did try to go and get in again. But he brought in men from somewhere else that done the work. They were supposed to hire a local man, but they brought in some of their own men from somewhere else to do the work that George Wilson could have done if that boss would have listened to him. But he was just a kid you might say, but he started in young on the road work and he knows. He knows how to drive tractors and run graders and all kinds of stuff. He wouldn't go back. He never went back to try to get in again. Then he got in on the railroad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One thing I was going to ask you about was what it was like, did you live in a section house on the railroad?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Yeah, I lived there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that like? What kind of accommodations were they? The section houses? What was the section house like?

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: To live in. Well, no.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: It's warm in here now. Hi, Mary. I'll leave that open now, Ed. Well, it was just about such a... not quite as good a house as this one is here in Nog.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: It was a common ordinary house and it hadn't been anyone in it much for quite a little while. It was pretty well run down when we moved there, but the company did put a roof on and put in some screen porches. It was only a four roomed house. They built a little bedroom onto the porch after we were there. There was a well just outside the back of the house. It was good as we'd never been used to. It was sealed with just lumber, beaded ceiling, we'd call it, you know, the walls. It was all lumber.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: We lived in some pretty sloppy places there, Jan.

speaker: Edward or Dixie Groseclose
words: Well, it was handy to work. You just had to walk across two tracks and he was on the motor car. So it was handy.
